Let  
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
with \(p=2017\).

We want
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

## 1) Use sum and difference
Let \(\chi(\cdot)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\), so on odd numbers in \(\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\),
\[
\chi(n)=
\begin{cases}
1,& n\in A,\\
-1,& n\in B.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
S_A+S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}=:T,
\]
and
\[
S_A-S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}=:D.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}
=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

So it remains to compute \(T\) and \(D^2\).

---

## 2) Compute \(T\)
The odd numbers are \(1,3,5,\dots,p-2\), so
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
T=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(D^2\)

Since \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), we have
\[
\chi(2)=1,\qquad \chi(-1)=1.
\]

Write each odd \(n\in\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\) as
\[
n=2j-p,\qquad j=\frac{p+1}{2},\dots,p-1.
\]
Then
\[
\chi(n)=\chi(2j-p)=\chi(2j)=\chi(j),
\]
and
\[
\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}
=\cos\left(\frac{(2j-p)\pi}{p}\right)
=\cos\left(\frac{2j\pi}{p}-\pi\right)
=-\cos\frac{2j\pi}{p}.
\]
Therefore
\[
D=-\sum_{j=(p+1)/2}^{p-1}\chi(j)\cos\frac{2j\pi}{p}.
\]
Now put \(j=p-k\). Since \(\chi(p-k)=\chi(-k)=\chi(k)\) and
\[
\cos\frac{2(p-k)\pi}{p}=\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p},
\]
we get
\[
D=-\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}.
\]

Now consider the quadratic Gauss sum
\[
\tau=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)e^{2\pi i k/p}.
\]
Because \(\chi(p-k)=\chi(k)\), the imaginary parts cancel, so
\[
\tau=2\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}.
\]
Hence
\[
D=-\frac{\tau}{2}.
\]
For a quadratic Gauss sum,
\[
\tau^2=p.
\]
Thus
\[
D^2=\frac{\tau^2}{4}=\frac{p}{4}.
\]
Since \(p=2017\),
\[
D^2=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

---

## 4) Finish
Now
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018/4}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}

Let  
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
with \(p=2017\).

We want
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

## 1) Compute \(S_A+S_B\)

Since \(A\cup B=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\), we have
\[
S_A+S_B=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using the standard sum
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
S_A+S_B=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{1}{2}.
\]
So
\[
T:=S_A+S_B=\frac12.
\]

## 2) Compute \(S_A-S_B\)

Let \(\chi(\cdot)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\). Then on the odd numbers,
\[
S_A-S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Call this quantity \(D\).

Use the indicator of odd integers:
\[
1_{n\text{ odd}}=\frac{1-(-1)^n}{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
D=\frac12\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}
-\frac12\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)(-1)^n\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

### First sum is \(0\)

Because \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), we have \(\chi(p-n)=\chi(n)\), while
\[
\cos\frac{(p-n)\pi}{p}=\cos\left(\pi-\frac{n\pi}{p}\right)=-\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
So the terms cancel in pairs, hence
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}=0.
\]

Therefore
\[
D=-\frac12\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)(-1)^n\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

Now use
\[
(-1)^n\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}
=\cos\left(n\pi-\frac{n\pi}{p}\right)
=\cos\left(\frac{(p-1)n\pi}{p}\right).
\]
Since \(\frac{p-1}{2}\) is an integer,
\[
\cos\left(\frac{(p-1)n\pi}{p}\right)
=\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{p}\cdot \frac{p-1}{2}\,n\right).
\]
Hence
\[
D=-\frac12\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\cos\left(\frac{2\pi kn}{p}\right),
\qquad k=\frac{p-1}{2}.
\]

## 3) Gauss sum evaluation

For nonzero \(k\pmod p\),
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)e^{2\pi i kn/p}=\chi(k)\tau,
\]
where for \(p\equiv1\pmod4\),
\[
\tau=\sqrt p.
\]
Also this sum is real, so
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\cos\left(\frac{2\pi kn}{p}\right)=\chi(k)\sqrt p.
\]
Thus
\[
D=-\frac12\chi\!\left(\frac{p-1}{2}\right)\sqrt p.
\]

Now
\[
\frac{p-1}{2}\equiv -\frac12 \pmod p,
\]
so
\[
\chi\!\left(\frac{p-1}{2}\right)=\chi(-1)\chi(2).
\]
Since \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod8\), we have
\[
\chi(-1)=1,\qquad \chi(2)=1.
\]
Therefore
\[
\chi\!\left(\frac{p-1}{2}\right)=1,
\]
and
\[
D=S_A-S_B=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}=-\frac{\sqrt{2017}}2.
\]

## 4) Finish

Use
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}
=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}.
\]
So
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\left(\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\right)^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

## 1) Express \(S_A,S_B\) using the Legendre symbol
Let \(\chi(n)=\left(\frac np\right)\), the Legendre symbol mod \(p\).  
Among the odd numbers \(1,3,5,\dots,p-2\),

- \(\chi(n)=1\) iff \(n\in A\),
- \(\chi(n)=-1\) iff \(n\in B\).

So define
\[
U=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\qquad
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A=\frac{U+T}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{U-T}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{U^2+T^2}{2}.
\]

So we only need \(U\) and \(T^2\).

---

## 2) Compute \(U\)
\[
U=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Use the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2mx)}{2\sin x}.
\]
Here \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), so
\[
U=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

Thus
\[
U=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(T\)

First consider the full sum
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Pair \(n\) with \(p-n\). Since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\), so
\[
\chi(p-n)=\chi(-n)=\chi(n).
\]
Also
\[
\cos\frac{(p-n)\pi}{p}=\cos\left(\pi-\frac{n\pi}{p}\right)=-\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
So each pair cancels, giving
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}=0.
\]

Split this into odd and even terms:
\[
T+\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}=0.
\]
Since \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod8\), we know
\[
\chi(2)=1.
\]
Therefore
\[
T=-\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]
So
\[
T^2=\left(\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}\right)^2.
\]

Now use the quadratic Gauss sum
\[
\tau=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)e^{2\pi i n/p}.
\]
For \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(\tau^2=p\), so \(|\tau|=\sqrt p\). Also \(\tau\) is real, and pairing \(n\) with \(p-n\) gives
\[
\tau=2\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]
Hence
\[
\left(2\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}\right)^2=p,
\]
so
\[
T^2=\frac p4.
\]

With \(p=2017\),
\[
T^2=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

---

## 4) Finish
Now
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{U^2+T^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

## 1) Split into total sum and character-weighted sum
Let \(\chi(n)=\left(\frac np\right)\) be the Legendre symbol modulo \(p\).  
On the odd numbers \(1,3,\dots,p-2\),

- \(\chi(n)=1\) iff \(n\in A\),

\[
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},

U=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},

then

S_A=\frac{T+U}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{T-U}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}.

So we only need \(T\) and \(U^2\).

---

## 2) Compute \(T\)
Write the odd numbers as \(2k+1\), \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). Then
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2mx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
T=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(U^2\)
Since \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod 8\), we have
\[
\chi(-1)=1,\qquad \chi(2)=1.
\]

Now every odd number in \(\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\) can be written as \(p-2m\) for \(m=1,2,\dots,\frac{p-1}{2}\). Thus
\[
U=\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(p-2m)\cos\frac{(p-2m)\pi}{p}.
\]
Because \(\chi(p-2m)=\chi(-2m)=\chi(-1)\chi(2)\chi(m)=\chi(m)\), and
\[
\cos\frac{(p-2m)\pi}{p}=\cos\left(\pi-\frac{2m\pi}{p}\right)=-\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p},
\]
we get
\[
U=-\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]

Now consider the quadratic Gauss sum
\[
G=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)e^{2\pi i m/p}.
\]
For a prime \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(G\) is real and \(|G|=\sqrt p\). Therefore
\[
\left(\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}\right)^2=p.
\]
Also, since \(\chi(p-m)=\chi(m)\) and \(\cos\frac{2\pi(p-m)}p=\cos\frac{2\pi m}p\), the full real sum is twice the half-sum:
\[
\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}
=2\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]
Hence
\[
4U^2=p,
\]
so
\[
U^2=\frac p4=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

---

## 4) Final computation
Therefore
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let  
\[
\chi(n)=\left(\frac{n}{p}\right)
\]
be the Legendre symbol mod \(p=2017\), and let
\[
O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}.
\]
Then for \(n\in O\),

- \(n\in A \iff \chi(n)=1\),
- \(n\in B \iff \chi(n)=-1\).

Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad
S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]

We will compute
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

## 1) Rewrite \(S_A,S_B\)

Since on odd \(n\), \(\frac{1+\chi(n)}2\) is the indicator of \(A\), and \(\frac{1-\chi(n)}2\) is the indicator of \(B\),

\[
S_A=\frac12\sum_{n\in O}(1+\chi(n))\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\]
\[
S_B=\frac12\sum_{n\in O}(1-\chi(n))\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

Let
\[
T=\sum_{n\in O}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},\qquad
U=\sum_{n\in O}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A=\frac{T+U}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{T-U}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2
=\frac{(T+U)^2+(T-U)^2}{4}
=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}.
\]

So it remains to compute \(T\) and \(U\).

---

## 2) Compute \(T\)

We need
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left((p-1)\pi/p\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
T=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(U\)

First consider the full sum
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Pair \(n\) with \(p-n\). Since \(p\equiv1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\), so
\[
\chi(p-n)=\chi(-n)=\chi(n).
\]
Also,
\[
\cos\frac{(p-n)\pi}{p}=\cos\left(\pi-\frac{n\pi}{p}\right)=-\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Thus each pair cancels, giving
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}=0.
\]

Split this into odd and even terms:
\[
U+\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ even}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}=0.
\]
So
\[
U=-\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]

Now \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod 8\), so
\[
\chi(2)=1.
\]
Hence
\[
U=-\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]

Now use the quadratic Gauss sum:
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)e^{2\pi i n/p}=\sqrt p
\qquad (p\equiv1\pmod4).
\]
Taking real parts,
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\cos\frac{2\pi n}{p}=\sqrt p.
\]
Again pair \(n\) with \(p-n\): both \(\chi\) and cosine are equal, so
\[
2\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}=\sqrt p.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
So
\[
U=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}=-\frac{\sqrt{2017}}2.
\]

---

## 4) Finish

Now
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\left(\frac{\sqrt{2017}}2\right)^2}{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}

Let  
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
where \(p=2017\).

We want
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

## 1) Write the sum and difference
Among the odd numbers \(1,3,5,\dots,p-2\), each is either a quadratic residue or a nonresidue, so define
\[
T=S_A+S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\]
and
\[
D=S_A-S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\]
where \(\chi(n)=\left(\frac np\right)\) is the Legendre symbol.

Then
\[
S_A=\frac{T+D}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{T-D}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(T+D)^2+(T-D)^2}{4}=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

So it remains to compute \(T\) and \(D^2\).

---

## 2) Compute \(T\)

Let \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\). Then
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2mx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left((p-1)\pi/p\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(D\)

Let
\[
O=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)e^{\pi i n/p},
\]
so that
\[
D=\Re(O).
\]

Now consider
\[
H=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)e^{\pi i n/p}=O+E,
\]
where
\[
E=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ even}}}\chi(n)e^{\pi i n/p}.
\]

### Claim: \(\Re(H)=0\)

Since \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\). Pair \(n\) with \(p-n\):
\[
\chi(p-n)=\chi(-1)\chi(n)=\chi(n),
\]
and
\[
e^{\pi i(p-n)/p}=e^{\pi i}e^{-\pi i n/p}=-e^{-\pi i n/p}.
\]
So each pair contributes
\[
\chi(n)\left(e^{\pi i n/p}-e^{-\pi i n/p}\right),
\]
which is purely imaginary. Thus \(\Re(H)=0\).

Therefore,
\[
D=\Re(O)=-\Re(E).
\]

### Now evaluate \(E\)

Write \(n=2m\). Then
\[
E=\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2m)e^{2\pi i m/p}
=\chi(2)\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)e^{2\pi i m/p}.
\]
Let
\[
U=\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)e^{2\pi i m/p}.
\]
Then \(\Re(E)=\chi(2)\Re(U)\).

But the quadratic Gauss sum is
\[
G=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)e^{2\pi i n/p}.
\]
Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we know
\[
G=\sqrt p.
\]
Also, pairing \(n\) with \(p-n\) gives
\[
G=2\Re(U).
\]
Hence
\[
\Re(U)=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
So
\[
D=-\Re(E)=-\chi(2)\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]

Now \(2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), so by the supplement to quadratic reciprocity,
\[
\chi(2)=\left(\frac2p\right)=1.
\]
Thus
\[
D=-\frac{\sqrt{2017}}{2},
\qquad\text{so}\qquad
D^2=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

---

## 4) Finish

Now
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018/4}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}

Let  
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
with \(p=2017\).

We want
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

## 1. First compute \(S_A+S_B\)

Since \(A\cup B=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\), we have
\[
S_A+S_B=\sum_{j=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2j+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using the standard identity
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{m-1}\cos((2j+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2mx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
S_A+S_B=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
S_A+S_B=\frac12.
\]

## 2. Compute \(S_A-S_B\)

Let \(\chi(\cdot)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\). Then on odd numbers,
\[
S_A-S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Call this quantity \(D\):
\[
D:=S_A-S_B.
\]

We will compute \(D\).

---

### 2a. A useful full sum is zero

Consider
\[
C:=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Let \(\xi=e^{i\pi/p}\). Then
\[
C=\Re\left(\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\xi^n\right).
\]
Now
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\xi^n
=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(p-n)\xi^{p-n}.
\]
Since \(p\equiv1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\), so \(\chi(p-n)=\chi(n)\). Also \(\xi^p=e^{i\pi}=-1\). Hence
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\xi^n
=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)(-\,\xi^{-n})
=-\overline{\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\xi^n}.
\]
So this sum is purely imaginary, and therefore its real part is \(0\). Thus
\[
C=0.
\]

---

### 2b. Compute the even part

Let
\[
E:=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ even}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
C=D+E=0,
\]
so \(D=-E\).

Now write \(n=2m\), where \(1\le m\le \frac{p-1}{2}\):
\[
E=\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]
Since \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod 8\), we have \(\chi(2)=1\). Therefore
\[
E=\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]
Because \(p\equiv1\pmod 4\), \(\chi(-1)=1\), so pairing \(m\) and \(p-m\),
\[
2E=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]
But
\[
\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)e^{2\pi i m/p}
\]
is the quadratic Gauss sum, which for \(p\equiv1\pmod 4\) equals \(\sqrt p\). Hence its real part is \(\sqrt p\), so
\[
2E=\sqrt p.
\]
Thus
\[
E=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
D=-E=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]

So
\[
S_A-S_B=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]

## 3. Now compute \(S_A^2+S_B^2\)

Use
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}.
\]
Substitute:
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\left(\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\right)^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

Let

S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},

with \(p=2017\).

We use the Legendre symbol \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\).  
On the odd numbers \(1,3,\dots,p-2\),

- \(\chi(n)=1\) iff \(n\in A\),
- \(\chi(n)=-1\) iff \(n\in B\).

So if we define
\[
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\qquad
U=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\]
then
\[
S_A=\frac{T+U}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{T-U}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}.
\]

So it remains to compute \(T\) and \(U^2\).

---

### 1) Compute \(T\)

\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using the standard sum,
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\cos((2k+1)\theta)=\frac{\sin(2m\theta)}{2\sin\theta},
\]
with \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\), \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

---

### 2) Compute \(U^2\)

Let
\[
M=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)e^{in\pi/p}.
\]
Then \(U=\Re(M)\).

Now define
\[
\tau=\sum_{\substack{x\ (\mathrm{mod}\ 2p)\\ x\text{ odd}}}\chi(x)e^{2\pi i x/(2p)}.
\]
Split the odd residues mod \(2p\) into:

- \(x=n\) for odd \(1\le n\le p-1\),
- \(x=p+n\) for even \(1\le n\le p-1\).

Thus
\[
\tau=\sum_{n\text{ odd}}\chi(n)e^{in\pi/p}
+\sum_{n\text{ even}}\chi(n)e^{i(n+p)\pi/p}
= \sum_{n\text{ odd}}\chi(n)e^{in\pi/p}
-\sum_{n\text{ even}}\chi(n)e^{in\pi/p}.
\]
Taking real parts,
\[
\Re(\tau)=
\sum_{n\text{ odd}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}
-\sum_{n\text{ even}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
But for \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(\chi(p-n)=\chi(n)\), while
\[
\cos\frac{(p-n)\pi}{p}=-\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\]
so
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}=0.
\]
Therefore the even-part real sum is \(-U\), and hence
\[
\Re(\tau)=2U.
\]

Now compute \(\tau\). For odd \(x\),
\[
e^{2\pi i x/(2p)}=-e^{2\pi i (x/2)/p},
\]
so
\[
\tau=-\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i (a/2)/p}.
\]
This is a quadratic Gauss sum:
\[
\tau=-\chi(2)\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i a/p}.
\]
Since \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod8\), we have \(\chi(2)=1\). Also for \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), the quadratic Gauss sum \(G\) satisfies
\[
G^2=p.
\]
Thus
\[
\tau^2=p.
\]
Moreover \(G\) is real here, so \(\tau\) is real, and
\[
(2U)^2=\Re(\tau)^2=\tau^2=p.
\]
Hence
\[
U^2=\frac p4.
\]

With \(p=2017\),
\[
U^2=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

---

### 3) Final computation

\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

## 1) Sum over all odd numbers

The odd numbers in \(\{1,2,\dots,p-1\}\) are
\[
1,3,5,\dots,p-2.
\]
So
\[
S_A+S_B=\sum_{j=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2j+1)\pi}{p}.

Using
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\cos((2j+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x},

with \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
S_A+S_B=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A+S_B=\frac12.
\]

## 2) Difference between residues and nonresidues

Let \(\chi(\cdot)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\).  
Then for odd \(k\in\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\),
- \(\chi(k)=1\) if \(k\in A\),
- \(\chi(k)=-1\) if \(k\in B\).

So
\[
S_A-S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\text{ odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
Call this quantity \(D\):
\[
D:=S_A-S_B.
\]

We will compute \(D\) using Gauss sums.

Let
\[
\zeta=e^{2\pi i/p},\qquad \eta=e^{\pi i/p}.
\]
Also let
\[
r=\frac{p+1}{2}.
\]
Since \(2r\equiv 1\pmod p\), and \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), we have
\[
\chi(r)=\chi(2)=1.
\]

Consider
\[
F:=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\text{ odd}}}\chi(k)\zeta^{rk}.
\]
Because oddness is selected by \(\frac{1-(-1)^k}{2}\),
\[
F=\frac12\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)(1-(-1)^k)\zeta^{rk}.
\]
Thus
\[
F=\frac12\left(\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\zeta^{rk}
-\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)(-1)^k\zeta^{rk}\right).
\]

Now:
- The first sum is a quadratic Gauss sum:
  \[
  \sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\zeta^{rk}=\chi(r)\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\zeta^k=\sqrt p,
  \]
  since \(\chi(r)=1\) and \(p\equiv1\pmod4\).
- Also \(\zeta^{rk}=(-1)^k\eta^k\), so
  \[
  \sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)(-1)^k\zeta^{rk}
  =\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\eta^k.
  \]
  This sum is purely imaginary, because \(p\equiv1\pmod4\) implies \(\chi(-1)=1\), and pairing \(k\) with \(p-k\) shows its real part is \(0\).

Therefore,
\[
\Re(F)=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]

But for odd \(k\),
\[
\zeta^{rk}=(-1)^k\eta^k=-\eta^k,
\]
so
\[
\eta^k=-\zeta^{rk}.
\]
Hence
\[
D=\Re\left(\sum_{k\text{ odd}}\chi(k)\eta^k\right)
=-\Re(F)
=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
D^2=\frac p4.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(S_A^2+S_B^2\)

Using
\[
S_A+S_B=\frac12,\qquad S_A-S_B=D,
\]
we have
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}.
\]
So
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

Define
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad
S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p}.
\]

We will compute \(S_A+S_B\) and \(S_A-S_B\).

---

## 1) Express \(S_A,S_B\) using \(\chi\)

Among the odd numbers \(1,3,5,\dots,p-2\), membership in \(A\) or \(B\) is determined by \(\chi(a)=\pm1\). So
\[
S_A=\frac12\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\text{ odd}}}(1+\chi(a))\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},
\]
\[
S_B=\frac12\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\text{ odd}}}(1-\chi(a))\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]

Hence if we set
\[
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad
U=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},
\]
then
\[
S_A=\frac{T+U}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{T-U}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(T+U)^2+(T-U)^2}{4}
=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}.
\]

So we only need \(T\) and \(U\).

---

## 2) Compute \(T\)

The odd numbers are \(a=2k+1\) for \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). Thus
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
T=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(U\)

Let
\[
\zeta=e^{2\pi i/p},\qquad \xi=e^{\pi i/p}.
\]
Then
\[
U=\Re\left(\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\xi^a\right).
\]

Now let
\[
t=\frac{p+1}{2}.
\]
Then \(2t=p+1\), so
\[
e^{2\pi i ta/p}=e^{\pi i a}e^{\pi i a/p}=(-1)^a\xi^a.
\]
For odd \(a\), \((-1)^a=-1\), hence
\[
\xi^a=-e^{2\pi i ta/p}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\sum_{a\text{ odd}}\chi(a)\xi^a
=
-\sum_{a\text{ odd}}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i ta/p}.
\]

Now use the quadratic Gauss sum:
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i ta/p}
=
\chi(t)\sqrt p.
\]
Since \(t\equiv 2^{-1}\pmod p\) and \(\chi(2)=1\), we have \(\chi(t)=1\). So
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i ta/p}=\sqrt p.
\]

Split this into odd and even \(a\):
\[
\sqrt p=\sum_{a\text{ odd}}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i ta/p}
+\sum_{a\text{ even}}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i ta/p}.
\]
Thus
\[
\sum_{a\text{ odd}}\chi(a)\xi^a
=
-\sqrt p+\sum_{a\text{ even}}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i ta/p}.
\]

For even \(a=2m\) (\(1\le m\le \frac{p-1}{2}\)),
\[
\chi(a)=\chi(2m)=\chi(2)\chi(m)=\chi(m),
\]
and
\[
e^{2\pi i t(2m)/p}=e^{2\pi i m(p+1)/p}=e^{2\pi i m/p}=\zeta^m.
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{a\text{ even}}\chi(a)e^{2\pi i ta/p}
=
\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\zeta^m=:H.
\]

So
\[
\sum_{a\text{ odd}}\chi(a)\xi^a=H-\sqrt p.
\]
Taking real parts,
\[
U=\Re(H)-\sqrt p.
\]

Now compute \(\Re(H)\). The full Gauss sum at \(1\) is
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\zeta^a=\sqrt p.
\]
But since \(\chi(-1)=1\),
\[
\sum_{a=1}^{p-1}\chi(a)\zeta^a
=
\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\zeta^m
+
\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(p-m)\zeta^{p-m}
=
H+\overline{H}=2\Re(H).
\]
So
\[
2\Re(H)=\sqrt p \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \Re(H)=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
U=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}-\sqrt p=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]

Thus
\[
U^2=\frac p4.
\]

---

## 4) Finish

We found
\[
T=\frac12,\qquad U^2=\frac p4.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let  
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
with \(p=2017\).

We use the Legendre symbol \(\chi(n)=\left(\frac np\right)\), so for odd \(n\in\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\),

- \(\chi(n)=1\) if \(n\in A\),
- \(\chi(n)=-1\) if \(n\in B\).

Then
\[
S_A+S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\]
and
\[
S_A-S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

So
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}.
\]

We compute these two quantities separately.

---

### 1) Compute \(S_A+S_B\)

Let
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{\frac{p-3}{2}}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using the standard sum
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2mx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{1}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A+S_B=\frac12.
\]

---

### 2) Compute \(S_A-S_B\)

Let
\[
D=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

Now set
\[
k=\frac{p+1}{2}.
\]
Since \(2k\equiv 1\pmod p\), consider the quadratic Gauss sum
\[
G(k)=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)e^{2\pi i kn/p}.
\]
Because \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-n)=\chi(n)\), so pairing \(n\) and \(p-n\),
\[
G(k)=2\sum_{n=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(n)\cos\frac{2\pi kn}{p}.
\]
But
\[
\frac{2\pi kn}{p}=\pi n+\frac{\pi n}{p},
\]
so
\[
\cos\frac{2\pi kn}{p}=(-1)^n\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Thus
\[
G(k)=2\sum_{n=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(n)(-1)^n\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

On the other hand, from each pair \(\{n,p-n\}\), exactly one is odd, and its contribution to \(D\) is
\[
(-1)^{n+1}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Therefore
\[
D=-\sum_{n=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(n)(-1)^n\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\]
so
\[
G(k)=-2D.
\]

Now by the Gauss sum evaluation,
\[
G(k)=\chi(k)\tau,\qquad \tau^2=\chi(-1)p=p
\]
since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\). Hence
\[
G(k)^2=p.
\]
Therefore
\[
D^2=\frac{G(k)^2}{4}=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

So
\[
(S_A-S_B)^2=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

---

### 3) Put it together

\[
S_A^2+S_B^2
=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let  
\[
x=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad y=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
where \(p=2017\).

We want
\[
x^2+y^2.
\]

## 1) Compute \(x+y\)

Since \(A\cup B=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\), we have
\[
x+y=\sum_{\substack{1\le t\le p-2\\ t\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{t\pi}{p}.
\]
This is
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2j+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using the standard sum of cosines in arithmetic progression,
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\cos((2j+1)\theta)=\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{2\sin\theta},
\]
with \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
x+y=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
x+y=\frac12.
\]

---

## 2) Compute \(x-y\)

Let \(\chi(\cdot)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\). Then for odd \(t\),
\[
\chi(t)=
\begin{cases}
1,& t\in A,\\
-1,& t\in B.
\end{cases}
\]
Hence
\[
x-y=\sum_{\substack{1\le t\le p-2\\ t\text{ odd}}}\chi(t)\cos\frac{t\pi}{p}.
\]

Now \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod4\), so
\[
\chi(-1)=1.
\]
Pair each odd \(t\) with the even number \(p-t\). Then
\[
\chi(p-t)=\chi(-1)\chi(t)=\chi(t),
\]
and
\[
\cos\frac{(p-t)\pi}{p}=\cos\left(\pi-\frac{t\pi}{p}\right)=-\cos\frac{t\pi}{p}.
\]
Therefore
\[
x-y=-\sum_{\substack{2\le s\le p-1\\ s\text{ even}}}\chi(s)\cos\frac{s\pi}{p}.
\]
Write \(s=2k\), \(1\le k\le \frac{p-1}{2}\). Since \(p\equiv1\pmod8\), we have
\[
\chi(2)=1,
\]
so \(\chi(2k)=\chi(k)\). Thus
\[
x-y=-\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}.
\]

Now use the quadratic Gauss sum:
\[
G=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)e^{2\pi i k/p}.
\]
For \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(G=\sqrt p\), so taking real parts,
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}=\sqrt p.
\]
Pairing \(k\) and \(p-k\) (which have the same \(\chi\) because \(\chi(-1)=1\)) gives
\[
2\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}=\sqrt p.
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
So
\[
x-y=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
In particular,
\[
(x-y)^2=\frac p4.
\]

---

## 3) Now compute \(x^2+y^2\)

Use
\[
x^2+y^2=\frac{(x+y)^2+(x-y)^2}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
x^2+y^2
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018/4}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let  
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
where \(p=2017\).

We want
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

## 1) Rewrite using the Legendre symbol
Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\).

Among the odd numbers \(1,3,5,\dots,p-2\),

- \(\chi(a)=1\) iff \(a\in A\),
- \(\chi(a)=-1\) iff \(a\in B\).

So define
\[
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=S_A+S_B,
\]
and
\[
D=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}=S_A-S_B.
\]

Hence
\[
S_A=\frac{T+D}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{T-D}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2
=\frac{(T+D)^2+(T-D)^2}{4}
=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

So we only need \(T\) and \(D^2\).

---

## 2) Compute \(T\)

\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]

Using the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
T=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(D\)

Since \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), we have
\[
\chi(2)=1.
\]
Also \(p\equiv1\pmod 4\), so
\[
\chi(-1)=1.
\]

Now for each odd \(a\in\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\), write
\[
a=2c-p,\qquad c=\frac{a+p}{2}.
\]
As \(a\) runs over odd numbers \(1,3,\dots,p-2\), \(c\) runs over
\[
\frac{p+1}{2},\frac{p+3}{2},\dots,p-1.
\]

Then
\[
\chi(a)=\chi(2c-p)=\chi(2c)=\chi(2)\chi(c)=\chi(c),
\]
and
\[
\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}
=\cos\frac{(2c-p)\pi}{p}
=\cos\left(\frac{2c\pi}{p}-\pi\right)
=-\cos\frac{2c\pi}{p}.
\]

So
\[
D=-\sum_{c=(p+1)/2}^{p-1}\chi(c)\cos\frac{2c\pi}{p}.
\]

Now pair \(c\) with \(p-c\). Because \(\chi(-1)=1\),
\[
\chi(p-c)=\chi(-c)=\chi(c),
\]
and
\[
\cos\frac{2(p-c)\pi}{p}=\cos\frac{2c\pi}{p}.
\]
Hence the sum over \(c=(p+1)/2,\dots,p-1\) equals the sum over \(c=1,\dots,(p-1)/2\). Therefore
\[
D=-\frac12\sum_{c=1}^{p-1}\chi(c)\cos\frac{2c\pi}{p}.
\]

Now consider the quadratic Gauss sum
\[
G=\sum_{c=1}^{p-1}\chi(c)e^{2\pi i c/p}.
\]
For prime \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(G\) is real and satisfies
\[
G^2=p.
\]
Thus
\[
\left(\sum_{c=1}^{p-1}\chi(c)\cos\frac{2c\pi}{p}\right)^2=p.
\]
So
\[
D^2=\frac{p}{4}=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

---

## 4) Finish

Now
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018/4}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let  
\[
X=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
Y=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
where \(p=2017\), and let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol.

Then for odd \(n\in\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\),

- \(n\in A \iff \chi(n)=1\),
- \(n\in B \iff \chi(n)=-1\).

So
\[
X=\frac12\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}(1+\chi(n))\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\]
\[
Y=\frac12\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}(1-\chi(n))\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

Hence
\[
X+Y=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\qquad
X-Y=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

We will compute these two quantities.

---

### 1) Compute \(X+Y\)

Write \(n=2j+1\), \(j=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). Then
\[
X+Y=\sum_{j=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2j+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{m-1}\cos((2j+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2mx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\), \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
X+Y=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
X+Y=\frac12.
\]

---

### 2) Compute \(X-Y\)

Let
\[
D:=X-Y=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Set \(\eta=e^{\pi i/p}\). Then
\[
D=\Re\left(\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\eta^n\right).
\]
Let
\[
T:=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\eta^n.
\]

Now define the Dirichlet character \(\psi\) modulo \(2p\) by
\[
\psi(n)=
\begin{cases}
\chi(n),& n\ \text{odd},\\
0,& n\ \text{even}.
\end{cases}
\]
Its Gauss sum is
\[
\tau(\psi)=\sum_{m=1}^{2p}\psi(m)e^{2\pi i m/(2p)}.
\]
A standard induced-character Gauss sum formula gives
\[
\tau(\psi)=\mu(2)\chi(2)\tau(\chi)=-\chi(2)\tau(\chi).
\]
Therefore
\[
|\tau(\psi)|=|\tau(\chi)|=\sqrt p.
\]

On the other hand, one checks by pairing odd and even residues that
\[
\tau(\psi)=2\Re(T)=2D.
\]
Thus
\[
|2D|=\sqrt p \quad\Longrightarrow\quad D^2=\frac p4.
\]

Since \(p=2017\),
\[
D^2=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

---

### 3) Compute \(X^2+Y^2\)

Use
\[
X^2+Y^2=\frac{(X+Y)^2+(X-Y)^2}{2}.
\]
So
\[
X^2+Y^2
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018/4}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

Therefore,
\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}.
\]

Let  
\[
\theta=\frac{\pi}{p},\qquad p=2017,
\]
and let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol modulo \(p\).

We want
\[
\left(\sum_{a\in A}\cos(a\theta)\right)^2+\left(\sum_{b\in B}\cos(b\theta)\right)^2.
\]

## 1) Introduce the two basic sums
Let
\[
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos(n\theta),
\]
and
\[
U=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos(n\theta).
\]

Since \(A\) consists of odd quadratic residues and \(B\) of odd nonresidues, we have
\[
\sum_{a\in A}\cos(a\theta)=\frac{T+U}{2},\qquad
\sum_{b\in B}\cos(b\theta)=\frac{T-U}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\left(\sum_{a\in A}\cos(a\theta)\right)^2+\left(\sum_{b\in B}\cos(b\theta)\right)^2
=\frac{(T+U)^2+(T-U)^2}{4}
=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}.
\]

So it remains to compute \(T\) and \(U^2\).

---

## 2) Compute \(T\)

The odd numbers from \(1\) to \(p-2\) are \(1,3,5,\dots,p-2\), so
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos((2k+1)\theta).
\]
Using the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2mx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\theta\), we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin((p-1)\theta)}{2\sin\theta}
=\frac{\sin\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{1}{2}.
\]

So
\[
T=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(U\)

Define
\[
F(z)=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)z^n.
\]
Then the odd-part sum is
\[
\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\,\xi^n
=\frac{F(\xi)-F(-\xi)}{2},
\qquad \xi=e^{i\pi/p}.
\]
Hence
\[
U=\Re\left(\frac{F(\xi)-F(-\xi)}{2}\right).
\]

### Step 3a: Show \(\Re(F(\xi))=0\)

Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\). Pair \(n\) with \(p-n\):
\[
\chi(p-n)=\chi(-1)\chi(n)=\chi(n),
\]
and
\[
\xi^{p-n}=\xi^p\xi^{-n}=-\xi^{-n}.
\]
Thus
\[
\chi(n)\xi^n+\chi(p-n)\xi^{p-n}
=\chi(n)(\xi^n-\xi^{-n}),
\]
which is purely imaginary. Summing over all pairs gives
\[
\Re(F(\xi))=0.
\]

### Step 3b: Compute \(F(-\xi)\)

Now
\[
-\xi=e^{i\pi}e^{i\pi/p}=e^{2\pi i\cdot \frac{p+1}{2p}}
=e^{2\pi i h/p},
\quad h=\frac{p+1}{2}.
\]
So
\[
F(-\xi)=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)e^{2\pi i hn/p}.
\]
This is a twisted quadratic Gauss sum:
\[
F(-\xi)=\chi(h)\tau,
\]
where
\[
\tau=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)e^{2\pi i n/p}.
\]
Since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(\tau=\sqrt p\).

Also \(h\equiv 2^{-1}\pmod p\), so
\[
\chi(h)=\chi(2^{-1})=\chi(2).
\]
Because \(2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), \(2\) is a quadratic residue mod \(p\), hence
\[
\chi(2)=1.
\]
Therefore
\[
F(-\xi)=\sqrt p.
\]

### Step 3c: Finish \(U\)

Thus
\[
U=\Re\left(\frac{F(\xi)-F(-\xi)}{2}\right)
=\frac{0-\sqrt p}{2}
=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
So
\[
U^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

---

## 4) Final computation

Now
\[
\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{p}{4}}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let  
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
with \(p=2017\).

We want
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

## 1) Express in terms of sum and difference
Let
\[
T=S_A+S_B,\qquad D=S_A-S_B.
\]
Then
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}
=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

So we just need \(T\) and \(D^2\).

---

## 2) Compute \(T\)

Since \(A\cup B=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\),
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Use the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)\theta)=\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{2\sin\theta}.
\]
Here \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\). Thus
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{1}{2}.
\]

So
\[
T=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(D\)

Let \(\chi(\cdot)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\). Then on odd numbers,
\[
D=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-2\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]

Because \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\). Also \(2017\equiv1\pmod 8\), so \(\chi(2)=1\).

Now pair odd and even residues via \(a\leftrightarrow p-a\). Since
\[
\cos\frac{(p-a)\pi}{p}=\cos\left(\pi-\frac{a\pi}{p}\right)=-\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},
\]
and
\[
\chi(p-a)=\chi(-a)=\chi(-1)\chi(a)=\chi(a),
\]
the total sum over all nonzero residues is \(0\):
\[
\sum_{x=1}^{p-1}\chi(x)\cos\frac{x\pi}{p}=0.
\]
Hence the odd-part sum is the negative of the even-part sum:
\[
D=-\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]
Since \(\chi(2)=1\),
\[
D=-\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]

Now consider the quadratic Gauss sum
\[
G=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)e^{2\pi i m/p}.
\]
Because \(\chi(-1)=1\), this is real, and
\[
G=2\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\cos\frac{2m\pi}{p}.
\]
Thus
\[
D=-\frac{G}{2}.
\]

For a prime \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), the quadratic Gauss sum satisfies
\[
G^2=p.
\]
Therefore
\[
D^2=\frac{G^2}{4}=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

So here
\[
D^2=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

---

## 4) Finish

Now
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018/4}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}

\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
where \(p=2017\).

We want
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

## 1) Sum over all odd numbers
The sets \(A\) and \(B\) partition the odd numbers
\[
1,3,5,\dots,p-2.
\]
So
\[
S_A+S_B=\sum_{j=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2j+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\cos((2j+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
S_A+S_B
=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
S_A+S_B=\frac12.
\]

## 2) Introduce the Legendre symbol
Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\), the quadratic character mod \(p\).  
Then on the odd numbers \(1,3,\dots,p-2\),
\[
\chi(a)=
\begin{cases}
1,& a\in A,\\
-1,& a\in B.
\end{cases}
\]
Hence
\[
S_A-S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}.
\]
Call this quantity \(D\). Then
\[
D=S_A-S_B.
\]

## 3) Compute \(D\) via a Gauss sum
Consider
\[
G=\sum_{\substack{1\le m\le 2p-1\\ m\text{ odd}}}\chi(m)e^{\pi i m/p}.
\]
Since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\). Pair the terms \(m=a\) and \(m=2p-a\) for odd \(a<p\):
\[
\chi(2p-a)=\chi(-a)=\chi(a),
\]
and
\[
e^{\pi i(2p-a)/p}=e^{-\pi ia/p}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\left(e^{\pi ia/p}+e^{-\pi ia/p}\right)
=2\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le p-1\\ a\text{ odd}}}\chi(a)\cos\frac{a\pi}{p}
=2D.
\]

So \(G=2D\).

Now reindex \(G\) by residues \(r=1,\dots,p-1\). For each \(r\), the unique odd representative of \(2r\pmod p\) in \(\{1,2,\dots,2p-1\}\) contributes
\[
\chi(2r)\cdot\bigl(-e^{2\pi ir/p}\bigr).
\]
Hence
\[
G=-\chi(2)\sum_{r=1}^{p-1}\chi(r)e^{2\pi ir/p}.
\]
The sum on the right is the quadratic Gauss sum \(\tau\). Since \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod8\), we have \(\chi(2)=1\). Thus
\[
G=-\tau.
\]

For a quadratic Gauss sum when \(p\equiv1\pmod4\),
\[
\tau^2=p.
\]
Therefore
\[
G^2=p.
\]
Since \(G=2D\),
\[
4D^2=p \quad\Rightarrow\quad D^2=\frac p4.
\]

So
\[
(S_A-S_B)^2=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

## 4) Finish
Now use
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}.
\]
Substitute:
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018/4}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}

Let  
\[
X=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
Y=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
where \(p=2017\).

We want
\[
X^2+Y^2.
\]

## 1) Express in terms of total sum and residue-character sum
Let \(\chi(\cdot)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\).  
On the odd numbers \(1,3,\dots,p-2\),

- \(\chi(k)=1\) for \(k\in A\),
- \(\chi(k)=-1\) for \(k\in B\).

So
\[
X+Y=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{k\pi}{p},
\]
and
\[
X-Y=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\text{ odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]

Hence
\[
X^2+Y^2=\frac{(X+Y)^2+(X-Y)^2}{2}.
\]

So we compute these two quantities.

---

## 2) Compute \(X+Y\)

We need
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2j+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using the standard cosine AP sum,
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\cos((2j+1)\theta)=\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{2\sin\theta}.
\]
Here \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\), \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\). Thus
\[
X+Y
=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
X+Y=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(X-Y\)

Let
\[
U:=X-Y=\sum_{\substack{1\le k\le p-1\\ k\text{ odd}}}\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]

Now consider
\[
V:=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}(-1)^k\chi(k)\cos\frac{k\pi}{p}.
\]
Since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\). Pair each even \(k\) with \(p-k\), which is odd. Then the even part equals \(-U\), and the odd part is also \(-U\). Therefore
\[
V=-2U.
\]

Now write this with roots of unity. Let \(\omega=e^{2\pi i/p}\). Since
\[
(-1)^k e^{ik\pi/p}=e^{2\pi i k\frac{p+1}{2p}}=\omega^{k(p+1)/2},
\]
we get
\[
V=\Re\left(\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^{k(p+1)/2}\right).
\]
By the quadratic Gauss sum formula,
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\omega^{mk}=\chi(m)\tau,
\]
where \(\tau\) is the quadratic Gauss sum and \(\tau^2=p\) because \(p\equiv1\pmod4\). Hence the above sum has square \(p\), and since it is real here,
\[
V^2=p.
\]
Thus
\[
(-2U)^2=p \quad\Rightarrow\quad U^2=\frac p4.
\]

So
\[
(X-Y)^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

---

## 4) Finish

Now
\[
X^2+Y^2
=\frac{(X+Y)^2+(X-Y)^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
X^2+Y^2=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}

Let  
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
where \(p=2017\).

We will compute
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

## 1) Sum over all odd numbers
Let
\[
O=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}.
\]
Then \(A\cup B=O\), so
\[
S_A+S_B=\sum_{n\in O}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Write \(n=2k+1\) with \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). Using
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2mx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
S_A+S_B
=\frac{\sin\left((p-1)\pi/p\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]
So let
\[
T:=S_A+S_B=\frac12.
\]

## 2) Difference using the Legendre symbol
Let \(\chi(n)=\left(\frac np\right)\) be the Legendre symbol.  
Then for odd \(n\in O\),

- \(\chi(n)=1\) if \(n\in A\),
- \(\chi(n)=-1\) if \(n\in B\).

Hence
\[
S_A-S_B=\sum_{n\in O}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Let
\[
D:=S_A-S_B.
\]

Now every odd \(n\in O\) can be written as
\[
n=p-2k,\qquad k=1,2,\dots,\frac{p-1}{2}.
\]
So
\[
D=\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(p-2k)\cos\frac{(p-2k)\pi}{p}.
\]

Since \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod 8\),

- \(\chi(-1)=1\) (because \(p\equiv1\pmod4\)),
- \(\chi(2)=1\) (because \(p\equiv\pm1\pmod8\)).

Thus
\[
\chi(p-2k)=\chi(-2k)=\chi(-1)\chi(2)\chi(k)=\chi(k).
\]
Also
\[
\cos\frac{(p-2k)\pi}{p}=\cos\left(\pi-\frac{2k\pi}{p}\right)=-\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}.
\]
Therefore
\[
D=-\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}.
\]

## 3) Use the quadratic Gauss sum
Consider
\[
G=\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)e^{2\pi i k/p}.
\]
Because \(\chi(p-k)=\chi(k)\) and \(e^{2\pi i(p-k)/p}=\overline{e^{2\pi ik/p}}\), we have
\[
G=2\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}.
\]
So
\[
D=-\frac G2.
\]

For the quadratic Gauss sum,
\[
G^2=\chi(-1)\,p=p
\]
since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\). Hence
\[
D^2=\frac{G^2}{4}=\frac p4.
\]

## 4) Compute \(S_A^2+S_B^2\)
Since
\[
S_A=\frac{T+D}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{T-D}{2},
\]
we get
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2
=\frac{(T+D)^2+(T-D)^2}{4}
=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}.
\]
Now \(T=\frac12\) and \(D^2=\frac p4\), so
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2
=\frac{\frac14+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}

Let  
\[
X=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad Y=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
where \(p=2017\).

We want
\[
X^2+Y^2.
\]

We will compute \(X+Y\) and \(X-Y\).

---

## 1) Compute \(X+Y\)

Since \(A\cup B=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\), we have
\[
X+Y=\sum_{k=0}^{\frac{p-3}{2}}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)\theta)=\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{2\sin\theta},
\]
with \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\),
\[
X+Y=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
X+Y=\frac12.
\]

---

## 2) Compute \(X-Y\)

Let \(\chi(\cdot)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\). Then for odd \(m\in\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\),
- \(\chi(m)=1\) if \(m\in A\),
- \(\chi(m)=-1\) if \(m\in B\).

Hence
\[
X-Y=\sum_{\substack{1\le m\le p-1\\ m\text{ odd}}}\chi(m)\cos\frac{m\pi}{p}.
\]

Define
\[
O=\sum_{\substack{1\le m\le p-1\\ m\text{ odd}}}\chi(m)e^{i m\pi/p},\qquad
E=\sum_{\substack{1\le m\le p-1\\ m\text{ even}}}\chi(m)e^{i m\pi/p}.
\]
Then
\[
X-Y=\Re(O).
\]

Now consider \(O+E\):
\[
O+E=\sum_{m=1}^{p-1}\chi(m)e^{i m\pi/p}.
\]
Pair \(m\) with \(p-m\). Since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), we have \(\chi(p-m)=\chi(-m)=\chi(m)\). Thus
\[
\chi(m)e^{i m\pi/p}+\chi(p-m)e^{i(p-m)\pi/p}
=\chi(m)\left(e^{i\theta}+e^{i(\pi-\theta)}\right)
=\chi(m)(e^{i\theta}-e^{-i\theta}),
\]
which is purely imaginary. Therefore \(O+E\) is purely imaginary, so
\[
\Re(O)=\Re\left(\frac{(O-E)+(O+E)}2\right)=\frac{\Re(O-E)}2.
\]
Thus
\[
X-Y=\frac{\Re(O-E)}2.
\]

So it remains to compute \(O-E\).

---

## 3) Evaluate \(O-E\)

Write
\[
O-E=\sum_{m=0}^{p-1}\chi(2m+1)e^{i(2m+1)\pi/p}.
\]
Let
\[
\omega=e^{2\pi i/p},\qquad \eta=e^{\pi i/p}.
\]
Then
\[
O-E=\eta\sum_{m=0}^{p-1}\chi(2m+1)\omega^m.
\]

Let \(r=\frac{p+1}{2}\), so \(2r\equiv1\pmod p\). As \(m\) runs mod \(p\), so does \(y=2m+1\), and \(m\equiv r(y-1)\pmod p\). Hence
\[
O-E=\eta\sum_{y\!\!\!\pmod p}\chi(y)\omega^{r(y-1)}
=\eta\omega^{-r}\sum_{y\!\!\!\pmod p}\chi(y)(\omega^r)^y.
\]
Now
\[
\omega^r=e^{2\pi i r/p}=e^{\pi i}e^{\pi i/p}=-\eta,
\]
so also \(\eta\omega^{-r}=-1\). Therefore
\[
O-E=-\sum_{y\!\!\!\pmod p}\chi(y)\omega^{ry}.
\]
By the standard property of Gauss sums,
\[
\sum_{y\!\!\!\pmod p}\chi(y)\omega^{ry}=\chi(r)\sum_{y\!\!\!\pmod p}\chi(y)\omega^y.
\]
Since \(r\equiv 2^{-1}\pmod p\), we get \(\chi(r)=\chi(2)\). And because \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod8\),
\[
\chi(2)=1.
\]
Thus
\[
O-E=-G,
\]
where
\[
G=\sum_{y\!\!\!\pmod p}\chi(y)\omega^y
\]
is the quadratic Gauss sum. Since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(G\) is real and satisfies
\[
G^2=p.
\]
So
\[
(O-E)^2=p.
\]
Because \(O-E\) is real,
\[
(X-Y)^2=\left(\frac{O-E}{2}\right)^2=\frac p4.
\]

Therefore
\[
(X-Y)^2=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

---

## 4) Finish

Now use
\[
X^2+Y^2=\frac{(X+Y)^2+(X-Y)^2}{2}.
\]
So
\[
X^2+Y^2
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}

Let

S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},

with \(p=2017\).

We will compute
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.

## 1) Introduce the Legendre symbol
Let \(\chi(x)=\left(\frac{x}{p}\right)\), so for odd \(x\in\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\),

- \(\chi(x)=1\) if \(x\in A\),
- \(\chi(x)=-1\) if \(x\in B\).

Define
\[
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le x\le p-1\\ x\ \text{odd}}}\cos\frac{x\pi}{p},
\qquad
U=\sum_{\substack{1\le x\le p-1\\ x\ \text{odd}}}\chi(x)\cos\frac{x\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A=\frac{T+U}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{T-U}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(T+U)^2+(T-U)^2}{4}=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}.
\]

So we only need \(T\) and \(U\).

---

## 2) Compute \(T\)

The odd numbers from \(1\) to \(p-2\) are \(1,3,\dots,p-2\), so
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Use the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)\theta)=\frac{\sin(2n\theta)}{2\sin\theta}.
\]
Here \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(\theta=\frac{\pi}{p}\). Thus
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
T=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(U\)

Write every odd number in \(\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\) as \(p-2k\), where \(k=1,2,\dots,\frac{p-1}{2}\). Then
\[
U=\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(p-2k)\cos\frac{(p-2k)\pi}{p}.
\]
Now:

- since \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), \(\chi(-1)=1\);
- since \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), \(\chi(2)=1\).

Therefore
\[
\chi(p-2k)=\chi(-2k)=\chi(-1)\chi(2)\chi(k)=\chi(k).
\]
Also
\[
\cos\frac{(p-2k)\pi}{p}=\cos\left(\pi-\frac{2k\pi}{p}\right)=-\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}.
\]
So
\[
U=-\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}.
\]

Now use the quadratic Gauss sum:
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)e^{2\pi i k/p}=\sqrt p
\qquad (\text{because }p\equiv 1\pmod 4).
\]
Taking real parts gives
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}=\sqrt p.
\]
Since \(\chi(p-k)=\chi(k)\) (again because \(\chi(-1)=1\)) and
\[
\cos\frac{2(p-k)\pi}{p}=\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p},
\]
the full sum is twice the half-sum:
\[
2\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}=\sqrt p.
\]
Hence
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(k)\cos\frac{2k\pi}{p}=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
U=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}=-\frac{\sqrt{2017}}{2}.
\]

---

## 4) Finish

Now
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\left(\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\right)^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
X+Y=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

Thus
\[
X+Y=\frac12.
\]

---

## 2) Compute \(X-Y\)

Let \(\chi(\cdot)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\). Then on odd numbers,
\[
\chi(n)=
\begin{cases}
1,& n\in A,\\
-1,& n\in B.
\end{cases}
\]
So
\[
X-Y=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Call this quantity \(D\):
\[
D:=X-Y.
\]

Now consider
\[
S:=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)(-1)^n\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Split into even and odd \(n\).

For even \(n\), write \(n=p-r\), where \(r\) is odd. Since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\), so
\[
\chi(p-r)=\chi(r),
\]
and
\[
\cos\frac{(p-r)\pi}{p}=\cos\left(\pi-\frac{r\pi}{p}\right)=-\cos\frac{r\pi}{p}.
\]
Hence the even part equals \(-D\). The odd part is also \(-D\) because of the factor \((-1)^n=-1\). Therefore
\[
S=-2D.
\]

---

## 3) Evaluate \(S\) via a Gauss sum

Let \(\zeta=e^{2\pi i/p}\). Then
\[
-e^{i\pi/p}=e^{i\pi}e^{i\pi/p}=e^{2\pi i\cdot \frac{p+1}{2p}}=\zeta^{(p+1)/2}.
\]
So
\[
S=\Re\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)(-e^{i\pi/p})^n
=\Re\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\zeta^{n(p+1)/2}.
\]
By the standard Gauss sum property,
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\zeta^{tn}=\chi(t)\,G,
\]
where
\[
G=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\zeta^n.
\]
Since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(G\) is real and \(G^2=p\), so \(|G|=\sqrt p\). Therefore
\[
S^2=p.
\]
Since \(S=-2D\),
\[
4D^2=p \quad\Rightarrow\quad D^2=\frac p4.
\]

Thus
\[
(X-Y)^2=\frac p4.
\]

---

## 4) Now compute \(X^2+Y^2\)

Use
\[
X^2+Y^2=\frac{(X+Y)^2+(X-Y)^2}{2}.
\]
So
\[
X^2+Y^2
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
X^2+Y^2=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let  
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
where \(p=2017\).

We will compute
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

## 1) Introduce the Legendre symbol
Let \(\chi(n)=\left(\frac np\right)\), the quadratic character mod \(p\).  
For odd \(n\in\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\),

- \(\chi(n)=1\) iff \(n\in A\),
- \(\chi(n)=-1\) iff \(n\in B\).

So define
\[
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\qquad
U=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A=\frac{T+U}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{T-U}{2}.
\]
Hence
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}.
\]

So we only need \(T\) and \(U\).

---

## 2) Compute \(T\)

The odd numbers are \(1,3,5,\dots,p-2\), so
\[
T=\sum_{j=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2j+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\cos((2j+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2Nx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(N=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}=\frac12.
\]

So
\[
T=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(U\)

Let
\[
\zeta=e^{2\pi i/p},\qquad h=\frac{p+1}{2}.
\]
Since \(2h=p+1\), for odd \(n\),
\[
\zeta^{hn}=e^{2\pi i hn/p}=e^{i\pi n}e^{i\pi n/p}=(-1)^n e^{i\pi n/p}=-e^{i\pi n/p}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}=-\Re(\zeta^{hn}) \qquad (n\text{ odd}),
\]
and thus
\[
U=-\Re\!\left(\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\zeta^{hn}\right).
\]
Let
\[
O=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\zeta^{hn}.
\]
Then \(U=-\Re(O)\).

### Total Gauss sum
Consider
\[
G=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\zeta^{hn}.
\]
Since multiplication by \(h\) permutes the nonzero residues mod \(p\),
\[
G=\chi(h)\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\zeta^n.
\]
Now \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod 8\), so \(\chi(2)=1\), hence \(\chi(h)=\chi(2^{-1})=\chi(2)=1\).  
Also for \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), the quadratic Gauss sum equals \(\sqrt p\). Thus
\[
G=\sqrt p.
\]

### Even part
Let the even part be
\[
E=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ even}}}\chi(n)\zeta^{hn}.
\]
Write \(n=2m\), \(1\le m\le \frac{p-1}{2}\). Then
\[
E=\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(2m)\zeta^{2hm}.
\]
Since \(\chi(2)=1\) and \(2h\equiv1\pmod p\),
\[
E=\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\zeta^m.
\]
Now
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\zeta^n
=
\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(m)\zeta^m
+
\sum_{m=1}^{(p-1)/2}\chi(p-m)\zeta^{p-m}.
\]
Because \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(\chi(p-m)=\chi(-m)=\chi(m)\), so the second sum is \(\overline{E}\). Hence
\[
\sqrt p = E+\overline{E}=2\Re(E),
\]
so
\[
\Re(E)=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]

Since \(G=O+E=\sqrt p\), we have
\[
\Re(O)=\sqrt p-\Re(E)=\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
U=-\Re(O)=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]
So
\[
U^2=\frac p4.
\]

---

## 4) Put it together

We found
\[
T=\frac12,\qquad U^2=\frac p4.
\]
Therefore
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
With \(p=2017\),
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let  
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
with \(p=2017\).

We want
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

## 1) Sum and difference

Let \(\chi(\cdot)\) be the Legendre symbol modulo \(p\).  
On the odd numbers \(1,3,5,\dots,p-2\),

- \(\chi(n)=1\) iff \(n\in A\),
- \(\chi(n)=-1\) iff \(n\in B\).

So define
\[
T:=S_A+S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\]
and
\[
D:=S_A-S_B=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

Then
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(T+D)^2+(T-D)^2}{4}
=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}.
\]

So we only need \(T\) and \(D^2\).

---

## 2) Compute \(T\)

The odd numbers are \(1,3,5,\dots,p-2\), so
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Use the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2nx)}{2\sin x}.
\]
Here \(n=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\). Thus
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin\left(\pi-\frac{\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{1}{2}.
\]

So
\[
T=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3) Compute \(D^2\)

Consider
\[
2D=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)(-1)^{n+1}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
This works because for each odd \(n\), the paired even number \(p-n\) has the same Legendre symbol and opposite cosine.

Now write cosine as real part. Let \(\zeta=e^{2\pi i/p}\), and let
\[
q=\frac{p+1}{2}.
\]
Since
\[
e^{2\pi i qn/p}=e^{i(\pi+\pi/p)n}=(-1)^n e^{in\pi/p},
\]
we get
\[
2D=-\Re\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\zeta^{qn}.
\]
By the Gauss sum property,
\[
\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\zeta^{qn}=\chi(q)\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)\zeta^n.
\]
The second sum is the quadratic Gauss sum \(G\), and since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\),
\[
G^2=p.
\]
Hence
\[
(2D)^2=p\quad\Longrightarrow\quad D^2=\frac p4.
\]

(Indeed here \(p=2017\equiv1\pmod8\), so \(\chi(2)=1\), but only the square is needed.)

Thus
\[
D^2=\frac{2017}{4}.
\]

---

## 4) Final computation

Now
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac{2017}{4}}{2}
=\frac{2018/4}{2}
=\frac{2018}{8}
=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let  
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
with \(p=2017\).

We will compute
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

## 1. Introduce the Legendre symbol
Let \(\chi(n)=\left(\frac np\right)\), so \(\chi(n)=1\) if \(n\) is a quadratic residue mod \(p\), and \(-1\) otherwise.

Among the odd numbers \(1,3,\dots,p-2\),
\[
S_A=\frac12\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}(1+\chi(n))\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},
\]
\[
S_B=\frac12\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}(1-\chi(n))\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

Define
\[
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p},\qquad
U=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]
Then
\[
S_A=\frac{T+U}{2},\qquad S_B=\frac{T-U}{2}.
\]
So
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(T+U)^2+(T-U)^2}{4}
=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}.
\]

Thus we only need \(T\) and \(U\).

---

## 2. Compute \(T\)
The odd numbers are \(1,3,5,\dots,p-2\), so
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]
Using the standard identity
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2mx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with \(m=\frac{p-1}{2}\) and \(x=\frac{\pi}{p}\), we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac12.
\]

---

## 3. Compute \(U\)
Now
\[
U=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-1\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

Since \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), we have
\[
\chi(2)=1.
\]
Every odd \(n\in\{1,3,\dots,p-2\}\) can be written uniquely as
\[
n=2x-p,\qquad x=\frac{n+p}{2},
\]
where \(x\) runs through \(\frac{p+1}{2},\dots,p-1\).

Then
\[
\chi(n)=\chi(2x-p)=\chi(2x)=\chi(2)\chi(x)=\chi(x),
\]
and
\[
\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}
=\cos\frac{(2x-p)\pi}{p}
=\cos\left(\frac{2x\pi}{p}-\pi\right)
=-\cos\frac{2x\pi}{p}.
\]
Hence
\[
U=-\sum_{x=(p+1)/2}^{p-1}\chi(x)\cos\frac{2x\pi}{p}.
\]

Now \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), so \(\chi(-1)=1\). Therefore
\[
\chi(p-x)=\chi(-x)=\chi(x),
\]
and also
\[
\cos\frac{2(p-x)\pi}{p}=\cos\frac{2x\pi}{p}.
\]
So the sum over the upper half equals the sum over the lower half, giving
\[
U=-\frac12\sum_{x=1}^{p-1}\chi(x)\cos\frac{2x\pi}{p}.
\]

But
\[
\sum_{x=1}^{p-1}\chi(x)e^{2\pi i x/p}
\]
is the quadratic Gauss sum, and since \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), its value is \(\sqrt p\), a real number. Thus
\[
\sum_{x=1}^{p-1}\chi(x)\cos\frac{2x\pi}{p}=\sqrt p.
\]
Therefore
\[
U=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]

For \(p=2017\),
\[
U=-\frac{\sqrt{2017}}{2}.
\]

---

## 4. Final computation
Now
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+U^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\left(\frac{\sqrt p}{2}\right)^2}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]
Substitute \(p=2017\):
\[
\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let  
\[
S_A=\sum_{a\in A}\cos\frac{a\pi}{p},\qquad 
S_B=\sum_{b\in B}\cos\frac{b\pi}{p},
\]
with \(p=2017\).

We want
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2.
\]

A standard trick is to use
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{(S_A+S_B)^2+(S_A-S_B)^2}{2}.
\]

So we compute the two quantities
\[
T:=S_A+S_B,\qquad D:=S_A-S_B.
\]

---

## 1) Compute \(T=S_A+S_B\)

Since \(A\cup B=\{1,3,5,\dots,p-2\}\), we have
\[
T=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

Write \(n=2k+1\), \(k=0,1,\dots,\frac{p-3}{2}\). Then
\[
T=\sum_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2}\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{p}.
\]

Using the standard sum
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{m-1}\cos((2k+1)x)=\frac{\sin(2mx)}{2\sin x},
\]
with
\[
m=\frac{p-1}{2},\qquad x=\frac{\pi}{p},
\]
we get
\[
T=\frac{\sin\left(\frac{(p-1)\pi}{p}\right)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{\sin(\pi-\pi/p)}{2\sin(\pi/p)}
=\frac{1}{2}.
\]

So
\[
T=\frac12.
\]

---

## 2) Compute \(D=S_A-S_B\)

Let \(\chi(\cdot)=\left(\frac{\cdot}{p}\right)\) be the Legendre symbol mod \(p\).  
On odd numbers \(1,3,\dots,p-2\), we have:

- \(\chi(n)=1\) if \(n\in A\),
- \(\chi(n)=-1\) if \(n\in B\).

Hence
\[
D=\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}.
\]

Now consider the induced character \(\psi\) modulo \(2p\), defined by
\[
\psi(n)=
\begin{cases}
\chi(n), & (n,2p)=1,\\
0, & (n,2p)>1.
\end{cases}
\]

Its Gauss sum is
\[
\tau(\psi)=\sum_{n=1}^{2p}\psi(n)e^{2\pi i n/(2p)}
=\sum_{n=1}^{2p}\psi(n)e^{i n\pi/p}.
\]

A standard formula for induced Gauss sums gives
\[
\tau(\psi)=\mu(2)\chi(2)\tau(\chi).
\]
Since \(p=2017\equiv 1\pmod 8\), we have
\[
\chi(2)=\left(\frac{2}{p}\right)=1.
\]
Also \(p\equiv 1\pmod 4\), so the quadratic Gauss sum satisfies
\[
\tau(\chi)=\sqrt p.
\]
Thus
\[
\tau(\psi)=-\sqrt p.
\]

So \(\tau(\psi)\) is real, and
\[
\Re(\tau(\psi))=-\sqrt p.
\]

---

### Relate \(\Re(\tau(\psi))\) to \(D\)

Let
\[
F:=\sum_{n=1}^{p-1}\chi(n)e^{i n\pi/p}.
\]
Because \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), we have \(\chi(-1)=1\). Pairing \(n\) and \(p-n\),
\[
\chi(p-n)=\chi(-n)=\chi(n),
\]
and
\[
e^{i(p-n)\pi/p}=-e^{-in\pi/p}.
\]
So each pair contributes
\[
\chi(n)\left(e^{in\pi/p}-e^{-in\pi/p}\right)=2i\chi(n)\sin\frac{n\pi}{p},
\]
which is purely imaginary. Therefore
\[
\Re(F)=0.
\]

Now \(\tau(\psi)\) differs from \(F\) by changing the sign on the even terms, so
\[
\Re(\tau(\psi))
=2\sum_{\substack{1\le n\le p-2\\ n\text{ odd}}}\chi(n)\cos\frac{n\pi}{p}
=2D.
\]
Hence
\[
2D=-\sqrt p,
\qquad\text{so}\qquad
D=-\frac{\sqrt p}{2}.
\]

Therefore
\[
D^2=\frac{p}{4}.
\]

---

## 3) Put it together

Now
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{T^2+D^2}{2}
=\frac{\left(\frac12\right)^2+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{\frac14+\frac p4}{2}
=\frac{p+1}{8}.
\]

With \(p=2017\),
\[
S_A^2+S_B^2=\frac{2017+1}{8}=\frac{2018}{8}=\frac{1009}{4}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\frac{1009}{4}}
\]

Let  
\[
D=\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\}.
\]

We want the minimum possible value of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d.
\]

## 1. Structural form of the sets

Given
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=\gcd(d,n)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so
\[
A_d\subseteq A_n.
\]

Hence for all \(i,j\),
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

Now define \(B_m\) to be the set of elements whose “smallest” set is \(A_m\). Then the \(B_m\)'s are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{m\mid n} B_m.
\]
So
\[
n=|A_n|=\sum_{m\mid n}|B_m|.
\]
By the identity \(\sum_{m\mid n}\varphi(m)=n\), we get
\[
|B_m|=\varphi(m).
\]

For divisors of \(250\), the relevant block sizes are:
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\ \varphi(2)=1,\ \varphi(5)=4,\ \varphi(10)=4,\ \varphi(25)=20,\ \varphi(50)=20,\ \varphi(125)=100,\ \varphi(250)=100.
\]

So:
- \(A_1=B_1\)
- \(A_2=B_1\sqcup B_2\)
- \(A_5=B_1\sqcup B_5\)
- \(A_{10}=B_1\sqcup B_2\sqcup B_5\sqcup B_{10}\)
- \(A_{25}=B_1\sqcup B_5\sqcup B_{25}\)
- \(A_{50}=B_1\sqcup B_2\sqcup B_5\sqcup B_{10}\sqcup B_{25}\sqcup B_{50}\)
- \(A_{125}=B_1\sqcup B_5\sqcup B_{25}\sqcup B_{125}\)
- \(A_{250}=\) all eight blocks.

To minimize the maxima, we may assign the numbers \(1,2,\dots,250\) to the \(250\) elements of \(A_{250}\), and all other elements larger numbers.

Thus the problem becomes: **order these eight blocks**.

---

## 2. Precedence relations

If \(e\mid f\), then every \(A_d\) containing \(B_f\) also contains \(B_e\). So in an optimal labeling, all elements of \(B_e\) can be placed before all elements of \(B_f\).

Thus the blocks must respect:

- \(B_1\) before \(B_2,B_5\)
- \(B_2,B_5\) before \(B_{10}\)
- \(B_5\) before \(B_{25}\)
- \(B_{10},B_{25}\) before \(B_{50}\)
- \(B_{25}\) before \(B_{125}\)
- \(B_{50},B_{125}\) before \(B_{250}\)

Also each block may be placed contiguously, since only its last element matters for maxima.

So each block is a “job” of length \(\varphi(d)\), and \(\max A_d\) is exactly the completion time of job \(B_d\).

Hence we need to minimize the sum of completion times of these jobs under the above precedence constraints.

---

## 3. Optimal order of the blocks

Work from the end.

- \(B_{250}\) must be last.
- Just before that, the only eligible blocks are \(B_{50}\) and \(B_{125}\).  
  For two consecutive jobs of lengths \(p<q\), putting the shorter first is better:
  \[
  (T+p)+(T+p+q) < (T+q)+(T+q+p).
  \]
  Since \(20<100\), we put \(B_{50}\) before \(B_{125}\), so from the end we place
  \[
  \dots, B_{50}, B_{125}, B_{250}.
  \]

- Before \(B_{50}\), the eligible blocks are \(B_{10}\) and \(B_{25}\), with lengths \(4\) and \(20\). So
  \[
  B_{10}\text{ before }B_{25}.
  \]

- Before \(B_{10}\), the eligible blocks are \(B_2\) and \(B_5\), with lengths \(1\) and \(4\). So
  \[
  B_2\text{ before }B_5.
  \]

And \(B_1\) must be first.

Therefore the optimal forward order is
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

---

## 4. Compute the maxima

Using cumulative block sizes:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\max A_1 &= 1,\\
\max A_2 &= 1+1=2,\\
\max A_5 &= 1+1+4=6,\\
\max A_{10} &= 1+1+4+4=10,\\
\max A_{25} &= 1+1+4+4+20=30,\\
\max A_{50} &= 1+1+4+4+20+20=50,\\
\max A_{125} &= 1+1+4+4+20+20+100=150,\\
\max A_{250} &= 250.
\end{aligned}
\]

So the minimum possible sum is
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This is achievable, for example, by assigning:
- \(B_1=\{1\}\)
- \(B_2=\{2\}\)
- \(B_5=\{3,4,5,6\}\)
- \(B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\}\)
- \(B_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\}\)
- \(B_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\}\)
- \(B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\}\)
- \(B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}\)

(and then define \(A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n}B_d\)).

So the minimum is

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
m_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).
\]
We want the minimum of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
= m_1+m_2+m_5+m_{10}+m_{25}+m_{50}+m_{125}+m_{250}.
\]

## 1. Basic facts

Given
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
setting \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]
So each \(A_i\) has exactly \(i\) elements.

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|,
\]
so \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

Thus:
- \(A_1\subseteq A_2\subseteq A_{10}\subseteq A_{50}\subseteq A_{250}\),
- \(A_1\subseteq A_5\subseteq A_{25}\subseteq A_{125}\subseteq A_{250}\),
- and \(A_{25}\subseteq A_{50}\) since \(25\mid 50\).

Also, since \(A_d\) is a set of positive integers with \(d\) elements,
\[
m_d\ge d.
\]

## 2. Lower bounds from unions

For any two finite sets of positive integers \(X,Y\),
\[
|X\cup Y|\le \max(\max X,\max Y).
\]
So if we know \(|X\cup Y|\), we get a lower bound on the larger maximum.

### Pair \(A_2, A_5\)

\[
|A_2\cup A_5|
=|A_2|+|A_5|-|A_2\cap A_5|
=2+5-\gcd(2,5)=2+5-1=6.
\]
Hence
\[
\max(m_2,m_5)\ge 6.
\]
Since \(m_2\ge 2\), the minimum possible value of \(m_2+m_5\) is
\[
2+6=8.
\]

### Pair \(A_{10}, A_{25}\)

\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|
=10+25-\gcd(10,25)
=10+25-5
=30.
\]
So
\[
\max(m_{10},m_{25})\ge 30.
\]
Since \(m_{10}\ge 10\), the minimum possible value of \(m_{10}+m_{25}\) is
\[
10+30=40.
\]

### Pair \(A_{50}, A_{125}\)

\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|
=50+125-\gcd(50,125)
=50+125-25
=150.
\]
So
\[
\max(m_{50},m_{125})\ge 150.
\]
Since \(m_{50}\ge 50\), the minimum possible value of \(m_{50}+m_{125}\) is
\[
50+150=200.
\]

Also,
\[
m_1\ge 1,\qquad m_{250}\ge 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
\ge 1+8+40+200+250=499.
\]

So the sum is at least \(499\).

---

## 3. Construction achieving 499

We now show this bound is attainable.

Use disjoint blocks \(B_n\) of sizes \(\varphi(n)\), and define
\[
A_k=\bigcup_{d\mid k} B_d.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} |B_d|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)
=\gcd(i,j),
\]
so this always works.

For the divisors of \(250\), choose:

\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
(These sizes are \(1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100\), i.e. \(\varphi(1),\varphi(2),\dots,\varphi(250)\).)

For all other \(n\), choose disjoint \(B_n\) using integers \(>250\).

Then:

- \(A_1=B_1=\{1\}\), so \(m_1=1\).
- \(A_2=B_1\cup B_2=\{1,2\}\), so \(m_2=2\).
- \(A_5=B_1\cup B_5=\{1,3,4,5,6\}\), so \(m_5=6\).
- \(A_{10}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}=\{1,2,\dots,10\}\), so \(m_{10}=10\).
- \(A_{25}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\), so \(m_{25}=30\).
- \(A_{50}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50}=\{1,2,\dots,50\}\), so \(m_{50}=50\).
- \(A_{125}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125}\), so \(m_{125}=150\).
- \(A_{250}\) is the union of all these blocks, so \(A_{250}=\{1,2,\dots,250\}\), hence \(m_{250}=250\).

Thus
\[
m_1+m_2+m_5+m_{10}+m_{25}+m_{50}+m_{125}+m_{250}
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

Since we already proved the sum cannot be smaller, this is the minimum.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

M_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).

## 1. Structural facts about the sets

Since
\[
|A_i|=|A_i\cap A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i,
\]
we have \(|A_i|=i\) for every \(i\).

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then \(\gcd(i,j)=i\), so
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=i=|A_i|.
\]
Thus \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

In fact, for all \(i,j\),
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)},
\]
because \(A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\) and both sets have size \(\gcd(i,j)\).

---

## 2. Decompose into “new” elements

Let \(E_n\) be the set of elements that first appear in \(A_n\), i.e. the elements whose minimal index is \(n\). Then the \(E_n\) are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{d\mid n} E_d.
\]
So
\[
|A_n|=\sum_{d\mid n} |E_d|=n.
\]
By Möbius inversion (equivalently, \(\sum_{d\mid n}\varphi(d)=n\)),
\[
|E_n|=\varphi(n).
\]

For divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\), these sizes are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
n&1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
\varphi(n)&1&1&4&4&20&20&100&100
\end{array}
\]

So inside \(A_{250}\) there are exactly \(250\) elements, partitioned into these blocks.

---

## 3. Lower bound on the sum of maxima

The divisors of \(250\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

We use union-size constraints.

### Pair \(A_2, A_5\)

\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=|A_2|+|A_5|-|A_2\cap A_5|
=2+5-1=6.
\]
So by time \(\max(M_2,M_5)\), all 6 elements of \(A_2\cup A_5\) must have appeared:
\[
\max(M_2,M_5)\ge 6.
\]
Since \(M_2\ge 2\),
\[
M_2+M_5\ge 2+6=8.
\]

### Pair \(A_{10}, A_{25}\)

\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-5=30,
\]
so
\[
\max(M_{10},M_{25})\ge 30.
\]
Since \(M_{10}\ge 10\),
\[
M_{10}+M_{25}\ge 10+30=40.
\]

### Pair \(A_{50}, A_{125}\)

\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-25=150,
\]
so
\[
\max(M_{50},M_{125})\ge 150.
\]
Since \(M_{50}\ge 50\),
\[
M_{50}+M_{125}\ge 50+150=200.
\]

Also,
\[
M_1\ge 1,\qquad M_{250}\ge 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} M_d
\ge 1+8+40+200+250
=499.
\]

So the sum is at least \(499\).

---

## 4. Construction achieving 499

Assign the \(250\) elements of \(A_{250}\) the labels \(1,2,\dots,250\) in blocks:

\[

\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)
=\gcd(i,j),
\]
so the condition is satisfied.

Now compute maxima:

\[
\begin{aligned}
M_1&=1,\\
M_2&=2,\\
M_5&=6,\\
M_{10}&=10,\\
M_{25}&=30,\\
M_{50}&=50,\\
M_{125}&=150,\\
M_{250}&=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.

This matches the lower bound, so it is minimal.

Let  
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad(\forall i,j\ge1).
\]

We want the minimum possible value of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d.
\]

The divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

---

## 1. Basic structure of the sets

Since
\[
|A_i|=|A_i\cap A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i,
\]
we have
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then \(\gcd(i,j)=i\), so
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=i=|A_i|.
\]
Hence
\[
A_i\subseteq A_j \qquad (i\mid j).
\]

More generally, because \(A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\) and both have size \(\gcd(i,j)\), we actually get
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

---

## 2. Decompose into disjoint “blocks”

For each positive integer \(n\), let \(B_n\) be the set of elements whose smallest containing set is \(A_n\). Then the \(B_n\) are disjoint and
\[
A_m=\bigsqcup_{n\mid m} B_n.
\]

Taking sizes,
\[
m=|A_m|=\sum_{n\mid m}|B_n|.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n).
\]

So for the divisors of \(250\), the relevant block sizes are:
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\quad \varphi(2)=1,\quad \varphi(5)=4,\quad \varphi(10)=4,
\]
\[
\varphi(25)=20,\quad \varphi(50)=20,\quad \varphi(125)=100,\quad \varphi(250)=100.
\]

---

## 3. Turn it into an ordering problem

Only these 8 blocks matter for \(\max A_d\) when \(d\mid250\).

We may assume these 250 elements are exactly \(1,2,\dots,250\), arranged in some order among the 8 blocks. Also, we may assume each block is contiguous; compressing a block into a consecutive chunk ending at the same last position cannot increase any \(\max A_d\).

So each block is like a job of length \(\varphi(n)\), and if its completion time is \(C_n\), then
\[
\max A_d=\max_{n\mid d} C_n.
\]

---

## 4. The order must respect divisibility

If \(a\mid b\) but block \(B_b\) appears before block \(B_a\), swapping them cannot increase any \(\max A_d\):

- any \(A_d\) containing \(B_b\) also contains \(B_a\),
- sets containing \(B_a\) but not \(B_b\) only improve.

So an optimal order is a linear extension of divisibility.

For such an order, all divisors of \(d\) appear before \(B_d\), hence
\[
\max A_d=C_d.
\]
Therefore the target sum becomes
\[
\sum_{d\mid250} C_d.
\]

---

## 5. Among feasible orders, use shortest available block first

For two adjacent incomparable blocks \(X,Y\) of lengths \(p_X,p_Y\), swapping them changes \(\sum C_d\) by \(p_X-p_Y\). So the smaller one should go first.

Thus we schedule the available blocks by increasing size:

- \(1\) first,
- then \(2\) before \(5\),
- then \(10\) before \(25\),
- then \(50\) before \(125\),
- then \(250\).

So the optimal order is
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

Their lengths are
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100,
\]
so the completion times are
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{d\mid250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

---

## 6. Construction achieving it

Take
\[
B_1=\{1\},\quad B_2=\{2\},\quad B_5=\{3,4,5,6\},\quad B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\},
\]
\[
B_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\},\quad B_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\},
\]
\[
B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\},\quad B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\]
For all other \(n\), choose disjoint sets \(B_n\) of size \(\varphi(n)\) using integers \(>250\).

Now define
\[
A_m=\bigcup_{n\mid m} B_n.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{n\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(n)=\gcd(i,j),
\]
and the maxima for divisors of \(250\) are exactly
\[
1,2,6,10,30,50,150,250.
\]

So the minimum is

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
m_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).
\]
We want the minimum possible value of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d.
\]

## 1. Basic structure of the sets

Since
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Now if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=\gcd(d,n)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so \(A_d\subseteq A_n\).

Hence for all \(i,j\),
\[
A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j,
\]
and both sides have size \(\gcd(i,j)\), so actually
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

So among the divisors of \(250\), inclusion is exactly divisibility.

---

## 2. Decompose \(A_{250}\) into “new” blocks

The divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

Inside \(A_{250}\), define \(B_d\) to be the elements whose smallest containing set \(A_e\) (with \(e\mid 250\)) is \(A_d\). Then
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{d\mid n} B_d \qquad (n\mid 250).
\]

Their sizes are forced by Möbius inversion, equivalently by Euler \(\varphi\):
\[
|B_d|=\varphi(d).
\]
So:
\[
|B_1|=1,\quad |B_2|=1,\quad |B_5|=4,\quad |B_{10}|=4,
\]
\[
|B_{25}|=20,\quad |B_{50}|=20,\quad |B_{125}|=100,\quad |B_{250}|=100.
\]

---

## 3. Reduce to ordering these blocks

Only elements of \(A_{250}\) matter for the maxima \(m_d\), since every \(A_d\subseteq A_{250}\) for \(d\mid 250\). So we may label the elements of \(A_{250}\) by \(1,2,\dots,250\).

An optimal labeling can be taken so that each block \(B_d\) occupies consecutive labels; then \(m_d\) is just the finishing time of block \(B_d\) in some order of the 8 blocks.

Also, if \(a\mid b\), then every set containing \(B_b\) also contains \(B_a\). So putting \(B_a\) before \(B_b\) is never worse than the reverse. Thus an optimal order must respect divisibility.

Under such an order, \(m_d\) equals the completion time of block \(B_d\), because \(A_d\) consists exactly of the blocks \(B_e\) with \(e\mid d\), all of which appear no later than \(B_d\).

So the problem becomes:

- jobs \(B_d\) with processing times \(\varphi(d)\),
- precedence \(a\mid b \Rightarrow B_a\) before \(B_b\),
- minimize sum of completion times.

For two available incomparable blocks, putting the shorter one first decreases the sum of completion times. So we choose shortest available block each time.

---

## 4. Optimal order

Processing times:
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100
\]
for
\[
B_1,B_2,B_5,B_{10},B_{25},B_{50},B_{125},B_{250}.
\]

Respecting divisibility and always taking the shorter available block gives:
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

Cumulative completion times:
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

---

First note that
\[
|A_n|=|A_n\cap A_n|=\gcd(n,n)=n.
\]
So every \(A_n\) has exactly \(n\) elements.

If \(d\mid n\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=\gcd(d,n)=d=|A_d|,
\]
hence
\[
A_d\subseteq A_n.
\]

Now for any \(i,j\), since \(A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\) and both sets have size \(\gcd(i,j)\), we get
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

So each element \(x\) belongs to a collection of sets closed upward under divisibility and closed under gcd. Therefore there is a unique positive integer \(r=r(x)\) such that
\[
x\in A_n \iff r\mid n.
\]

Let \(B_r\) be the set of elements with this value \(r(x)=r\). Then the \(B_r\) are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{r\mid n} B_r.
\]

If \(c_r=|B_r|\), then
\[
|A_n|=\sum_{r\mid n} c_r=n.
\]
By Möbius inversion, this forces
\[
c_r=\varphi(r).
\]

So for divisors of \(250\), the relevant block sizes are:

\[
\begin{aligned}
|B_1|&=\varphi(1)=1,\\
|B_2|&=\varphi(2)=1,\\
|B_5|&=\varphi(5)=4,\\
|B_{10}|&=\varphi(10)=4,\\
|B_{25}|&=\varphi(25)=20,\\
|B_{50}|&=\varphi(50)=20,\\
|B_{125}|&=\varphi(125)=100,\\
|B_{250}|&=\varphi(250)=100.
\end{aligned}
\]

---

## 2. How to minimize the maxima

For each \(d\mid 250\),
\[
A_d=\bigcup_{r\mid d} B_r.
\]

If \(r\mid s\), then every set containing \(B_s\) also contains \(B_r\). So placing elements of \(B_r\) later than elements of \(B_s\) can never help; swapping them does not increase any \(\max A_d\). Thus we may assume all blocks are ordered compatibly with divisibility.
