Then for each divisor \(d\) of \(250\), block \(B_d\) is the last block among those making up \(A_d\), so \(\max A_d\) is exactly the completion time of block \(B_d\).

Hence we have a scheduling problem:

- job \(d\) has processing time \(\varphi(d)\),
- precedence \(r\prec s\) whenever \(r\mid s\),
- minimize sum of completion times.

For equal weights, among available jobs we should do the shortest one first: if two available consecutive jobs have lengths \(p>q\), swapping them reduces total completion time by \(p-q\).

So the optimal order is:
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

Their lengths are:
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100.
\]

Thus the completion times are:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\max A_1&=1,\\
\max A_2&=1+1=2,\\
\max A_5&=1+1+4=6,\\
\max A_{10}&=10,\\
\max A_{25}&=30,\\
\max A_{50}&=50,\\
\max A_{125}&=150,\\
\max A_{250}&=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

So
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

This is achievable, for example by assigning consecutive integers to the blocks in that order:
\[
B_1=\{1\},\ B_2=\{2\},\ B_5=\{3,4,5,6\},\ \dots,\ B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\]

Therefore the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
S=\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d,
\]
where the divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

We will find the minimum possible value of \(S\).

---

### 1. The sets must come from disjoint “blocks”

Since \( \gcd(i,j)\mid i,j\), we have \(A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\).  
But
\[
|A_{\gcd(i,j)}|=\gcd(i,j)=|A_i\cap A_j|,
\]
so in fact
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

Now fix an element \(x\). Let
\[
T_x=\{n:x\in A_n\}.
\]
Because intersections correspond to gcd, \(T_x\) is closed under gcd; and because \(A_d\subseteq A_n\) whenever \(d\mid n\), \(T_x\) is upward-closed under divisibility. Hence \(T_x\) has a unique minimal element, say \(m_x\), and then
\[
x\in A_n \iff m_x\mid n.
\]

So if we define
\[
B_m=\{x:m_x=m\},
\]
then the \(B_m\) are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{m\mid n} B_m.
\]
Taking sizes,
\[
n=|A_n|=\sum_{m\mid n}|B_m|.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
|B_m|=\varphi(m).
\]

For the divisors of \(250\), the relevant block sizes are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
m&1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
\varphi(m)&1&1&4&4&20&20&100&100
\end{array}
\]

---

### 2. Reduce to ordering these 8 blocks

Only the elements in \(A_{250}\) matter for our sum, and
\[
|A_{250}|=250.
\]
So we may label the elements of these 8 blocks using \(1,2,\dots,250\), and put everything else later.

Let \(t_m\) be the largest label used in block \(B_m\). Then
\[
\max A_d=\max_{m\mid d} t_m.
\]

We may assume each block is a consecutive interval, ordered by increasing \(t_m\). This does not increase any \(\max A_d\). So the problem becomes:

- arrange the 8 blocks in some order,
- each block \(B_m\) has length \(\varphi(m)\),
- \(\max A_d\) is the ending position of the last block among divisors of \(d\).

---

### 3. In an optimal order, divisors must come before multiples

Suppose \(m\mid n\), but block \(B_n\) is placed before \(B_m\). Swap these two adjacent blocks.

- Any \(A_d\) containing \(B_n\) also contains \(B_m\), since \(n\mid d\Rightarrow m\mid d\).
- So sets containing \(B_n\) are unaffected by the swap.
- Sets containing \(B_m\) but not \(B_n\) get their maximum reduced.

Thus swapping cannot increase the sum. Repeating, we may assume:

> If \(m\mid n\), then \(B_m\) appears before \(B_n\).

So the block order is a linear extension of divisibility.

In such an order, for each divisor \(d\), block \(B_d\) is the **last** among all blocks \(B_m\) with \(m\mid d\). Hence
\[
\max A_d=\text{ending position of block }B_d.
\]

Therefore we now need to minimize the sum of completion times of the 8 jobs with processing times \(\varphi(m)\), subject to divisibility precedence.

---

### 4. Among incomparable blocks, shorter should come first

If two adjacent blocks \(B_a,B_b\) are incomparable under divisibility, then swapping them preserves the precedence constraints. If their lengths are \(p_a,p_b\), their contribution to the sum of completion times is minimized by placing the shorter one first.

So in an optimal topological order, at each step we choose the available block with smallest \(\varphi(m)\).

Now do that:

- First must be \(1\) (length \(1\)).
- Then available: \(2,5\). Choose \(2\) (length \(1\)).
- Then only \(5\) is available (length \(4\)).
- Then available: \(10,25\). Choose \(10\) (length \(4\)).
- Then \(25\) (length \(20\)).
- Then available: \(50,125\). Choose \(50\) (length \(20\)).
- Then \(125\) (length \(100\)).
- Then \(250\) (length \(100\)).

So the optimal order is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

---

### 5. Compute the maxima

Using block lengths \(1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100\), the ending positions are:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\max A_1 &=1,\\
\max A_2 &=1+1=2,\\
\max A_5 &=2+4=6,\\
\max A_{10} &=6+4=10,\\
\max A_{25} &=10+20=30,\\
\max A_{50} &=30+20=50,\\
\max A_{125} &=50+100=150,\\
\max A_{250} &=150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus
\[
S=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This is achievable, for example, by taking the blocks as consecutive intervals:
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]

So the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
m_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).
\]
We want the minimum of
\[
m_1+m_2+m_5+m_{10}+m_{25}+m_{50}+m_{125}+m_{250}.
\]

## 1. Basic facts

Since
\[
|A_i|=|A_i\cap A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i,
\]
we have
\[
|A_d|=d \quad \text{for every } d.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=\gcd(d,n)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so
\[
A_d\subseteq A_n.
\]

In particular,
\[
A_1\subseteq A_2\subseteq A_{10}\subseteq A_{50}\subseteq A_{250},
\]
and
\[
A_1\subseteq A_5\subseteq A_{25}\subseteq A_{125}\subseteq A_{250}.
\]

---

## 2. A useful lemma

If \(X,Y\) are finite sets of positive integers with
\[
|X|=a,\quad |Y|=b,\quad |X\cap Y|=c,\quad a\le b,
\]
then
\[
\max X+\max Y\ge 2a+b-c.
\]

### Proof
Let \(M=\max X\), \(N=\max Y\).

- Since \(X\cup Y\subseteq \{1,2,\dots,\max(M,N)\}\),
  \[
  \max(M,N)\ge |X\cup Y|=a+b-c.
  \]
- Also \(M\ge a\) and \(N\ge b\ge a\), so
  \[
  \min(M,N)\ge a.
  \]

Thus
\[
M+N=\max(M,N)+\min(M,N)\ge (a+b-c)+a=2a+b-c.
\]
Proved.

---

## 3. Lower bound for the desired sum

Apply the lemma to these three pairs:

### Pair \(A_2,A_5\)
\[
|A_2|=2,\quad |A_5|=5,\quad |A_2\cap A_5|=\gcd(2,5)=1.
\]
So
\[
m_2+m_5\ge 2\cdot 2+5-1=8.
\]

### Pair \(A_{10},A_{25}\)
\[
|A_{10}|=10,\quad |A_{25}|=25,\quad |A_{10}\cap A_{25}|=\gcd(10,25)=5.
\]
So
\[
m_{10}+m_{25}\ge 2\cdot 10+25-5=40.
\]

### Pair \(A_{50},A_{125}\)
\[
|A_{50}|=50,\quad |A_{125}|=125,\quad |A_{50}\cap A_{125}|=\gcd(50,125)=25.
\]
So
\[
m_{50}+m_{125}\ge 2\cdot 50+125-25=200.
\]

Also,
\[
m_1\ge |A_1|=1,\qquad m_{250}\ge |A_{250}|=250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
\ge 1+8+40+200+250
=499.
\]

So the minimum is at least \(499\).

---

## 4. Construction achieving 499

Use the standard construction with disjoint blocks \(B_n\) of size \(\varphi(n)\), and define
\[
A_i=\bigcup_{d\mid i} B_d.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)
=\gcd(i,j).
\]

Now choose the blocks for divisors of \(250\) as follows:

\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
(For all other \(n\), choose disjoint \(B_n\) using integers \(>250\).)

Then:

- \(A_1=B_1\), so \(m_1=1\).
- \(A_2=B_1\cup B_2\), so \(m_2=2\).
- \(A_5=B_1\cup B_5\), so \(m_5=6\).
- \(A_{10}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\), so \(m_{10}=10\).
- \(A_{25}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\), so \(m_{25}=30\).
- \(A_{50}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50}\), so \(m_{50}=50\).
- \(A_{125}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125}\), so \(m_{125}=150\).
- \(A_{250}\) contains all these blocks, so \(m_{250}=250\).

Hence
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This matches the lower bound, so it is the minimum.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad(\forall i,j\ge 1).
\]

We want the minimum of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
\]
over the divisors \(d=1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\).

---

### 1. Basic structure of the sets

Since \(|A_i\cap A_i|=i\), we have
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|,
\]
so \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

Hence for all \(i,j\),
\[
A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j
\]
and both sides have size \(\gcd(i,j)\), so actually
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

---

### 2. “Types” of elements

For any element \(x\), let \(m(x)\) be the smallest index such that \(x\in A_{m(x)}\).

Because \(A_r\subseteq A_s\) whenever \(r\mid s\), an element with minimal index \(m\) lies in exactly those \(A_n\) with \(m\mid n\).

Let \(c_m\) be the number of elements with minimal index \(m\). Then
\[
|A_n|=\sum_{m\mid n} c_m=n.
\]
By Möbius inversion, this forces
\[
c_m=\varphi(m).
\]

So for each \(m\), there are exactly \(\varphi(m)\) elements whose minimal index is \(m\).

For divisors of \(250\), the relevant types are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
m & \varphi(m)\\ \hline
1 & 1\\
2 & 1\\
5 & 4\\
10 & 4\\
25 & 20\\
50 & 20\\
125 & 100\\
250 & 100
\end{array}
\]

These \(1+1+4+4+20+20+100+100=250\) elements are exactly the elements of \(A_{250}\).

---

### 3. Reduce to ordering 8 blocks

Group the \(250\) elements of \(A_{250}\) into 8 blocks by their minimal index.  
Call the block for divisor \(d\) by \(B_d\), with size \(\varphi(d)\).

For each divisor \(e\mid 250\), the set \(A_e\) consists exactly of the blocks \(B_d\) with \(d\mid e\).

Only the *largest label* used in each block matters for the maxima, so we may place each block consecutively.

---

### 4. Required precedence between blocks

If \(r\mid s\), then every \(A_e\) containing \(B_s\) also contains \(B_r\).  
So placing \(B_s\) before \(B_r\) is never better than swapping them to \(B_r\) then \(B_s\).

Thus in an optimal arrangement, blocks must respect divisibility:
\[
B_r \text{ comes before } B_s \quad \text{whenever } r\mid s.
\]

So we need a topological order of the divisibility poset on
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

Under such an order, the maximum of \(A_d\) is exactly the finishing position of block \(B_d\).

So the problem becomes:

> Order these 8 blocks, respecting divisibility, to minimize the sum of their completion times.

The block sizes are
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100.
\]

---

### 5. Choose the optimal topological order

The divisibility constraints are:

- \(1\) before \(2,5\)
- \(2,5\) before \(10\)
- \(5\) before \(25\)
- \(10,25\) before \(50\)
- \(25\) before \(125\)
- \(50,125\) before \(250\)

At each point, among currently available incomparable blocks, putting the shorter one first minimizes total completion time.

So:

- after \(1\), choose \(2\) before \(5\) since \(1<4\),
- after \(1,2,5\), choose \(10\) before \(25\) since \(4<20\),
- after \(1,2,5,10,25\), choose \(50\) before \(125\) since \(20<100\).

Hence the optimal order is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

---

### 6. Compute the maxima

Cumulative block sizes:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c}
d & \max A_d \\ \hline
1 & 1\\
2 & 1+1=2\\
5 & 2+4=6\\
10 & 6+4=10\\
25 & 10+20=30\\
50 & 30+20=50\\
125 & 50+100=150\\
250 & 150+100=250
\end{array}
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This is attainable by labeling the blocks in exactly that order.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad(\forall i,j\ge 1).
\]

We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
\]
over the divisors \(d\in\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\}\).

## 1. Basic structure

First note that
\[
|A_i|=|A_i\cap A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=\gcd(d,n)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so \(A_d\subseteq A_n\).

Hence for any \(i,j\),
\[
A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j,
\]
and both sides have size \(\gcd(i,j)\), so actually
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

## 2. A useful lower bound for two sets

If \(X,Y\) are finite sets of positive integers, then
\[
\max X+\max Y\ge |X\cup Y|+\min(|X|,|Y|).
\]
Reason: one of \(\max X,\max Y\) is at least \(|X\cup Y|\), and the other is at least the size of the smaller set.

We now apply this to three natural pairs.

---

### Pair \(A_2,A_5\)

Since
\[
A_2\cap A_5=A_1,\qquad |A_1|=1,
\]
we get
\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=|A_2|+|A_5|-|A_1|=2+5-1=6.
\]
Thus
\[
\max A_2+\max A_5\ge 6+2=8.
\]

### Pair \(A_{10},A_{25}\)

Since
\[
A_{10}\cap A_{25}=A_5,\qquad |A_5|=5,
\]
we get
\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-5=30.
\]
Thus
\[
\max A_{10}+\max A_{25}\ge 30+10=40.
\]

### Pair \(A_{50},A_{125}\)

Since
\[
A_{50}\cap A_{125}=A_{25},\qquad |A_{25}|=25,
\]
we get
\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-25=150.
\]
Thus
\[
\max A_{50}+\max A_{125}\ge 150+50=200.
\]

---

Also,
\[
\max A_1\ge |A_1|=1,\qquad \max A_{250}\ge |A_{250}|=250.
\]

So the total satisfies
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
\ge 1+8+40+200+250=499.
\]

## 3. Construction achieving 499

We now show this lower bound is attainable.

Take disjoint blocks:
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
These have sizes
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100,
\]
which are exactly
\[
\varphi(1),\varphi(2),\varphi(5),\varphi(10),\varphi(25),\varphi(50),\varphi(125),\varphi(250).
\]

For every \(n\), define
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d,
\]
where for \(d\nmid 250\) we choose further disjoint blocks \(B_d\) of size \(\varphi(d)\) using integers \(>250\).

Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} |B_d|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)
=\gcd(i,j),
\]
so the condition is satisfied.

For the needed divisors:
\[
\begin{aligned}
A_1&=\{1\}, &&\max A_1=1,\\
A_2&=\{1,2\}, &&\max A_2=2,\\
A_5&=\{1,3,4,5,6\}, &&\max A_5=6,\\
A_{10}&=\{1,2,\dots,10\}, &&\max A_{10}=10,\\
A_{25}&=\{1,3,4,5,6,11,\dots,30\}, &&\max A_{25}=30,\\
A_{50}&=\{1,2,\dots,50\}, &&\max A_{50}=50,\\
A_{125}&=\{1,3,4,5,6,11,\dots,30,51,\dots,150\}, &&\max A_{125}=150,\\
A_{250}&=\{1,2,\dots,250\}, &&\max A_{250}=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

So the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad(\forall i,j\ge1).
\]

We want the minimum of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d,
\]
where the divisors of \(250\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

## 1. Structural form of the sets

First, if \(i\mid j\), then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|,
\]
so \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

Also, for any \(i,j\), since \(\gcd(i,j)\mid i,j\), we have
\[
A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j.
\]
But both sets have size \(\gcd(i,j)\), so actually
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

Now each element \(x\) belongs to all \(A_n\) with \(n\) divisible by some unique minimal positive integer \(m\).  
Let \(B_m\) be the set of elements whose minimal index is \(m\). Then the \(B_m\) are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{m\mid n} B_m.
\]
Taking sizes,
\[
n=|A_n|=\sum_{m\mid n}|B_m|.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
|B_m|=\varphi(m).
\]

So for divisors of \(250\), the relevant blocks are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
m & 1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
|B_m|=\varphi(m) & 1&1&4&4&20&20&100&100
\end{array}
\]

Since every \(A_d\) with \(d\mid250\) lies inside \(A_{250}\), and \(|A_{250}|=250\), we may label the elements of \(A_{250}\) by \(1,2,\dots,250\), and all other elements by larger integers. Thus the problem is to order these 8 blocks.

## 2. Why the block order must respect divisibility

If \(m\mid n\) and \(m<n\), then every \(A_d\) containing \(B_n\) also contains \(B_m\). So if \(B_n\) is placed before \(B_m\), swapping them cannot increase any \(\max A_d\), and may decrease some. Hence in an optimal arrangement, whenever \(m\mid n\), block \(B_m\) comes before block \(B_n\).

Therefore the block order must be a linear extension of divisibility:
- \(1\) first,
- \(2\) before \(10\),
- \(5\) before \(10,25\),
- \(10,25\) before \(50\),
- \(25\) before \(125\),
- \(50,125\) before \(250\).

Under such an order, \(\max A_d\) is just the cumulative size up to block \(B_d\).

## 3. Choose the best linear extension

The block sizes are:
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100.
\]

We compare the only meaningful branch choices.

### Choice between \(2\) and \(5\)

- If we start \(1,2,5,\dots\), that leads to the candidate
  \[
  1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
  \]
- If we start \(1,5,\dots\), the best continuation is
  \[
  1,5,2,10,25,50,125,250,
  \]
  giving maxima
  \[
  1,6,5,10,30,50,150,250,
  \]
  whose sum is
  \[
  1+6+5+10+30+50+150+250=502.
  \]

So \(2\) should come before \(5\).

### Choice between \(10\) and \(25\) after \(1,2,5\)

- If we take \(10\) first, best continuation is
  \[
  1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
  \]
- If we take \(25\) first, the best continuation is
  \[
  1,2,5,25,10,50,125,250,
  \]
  giving maxima
  \[
  1,2,6,30,26,50,150,250,
  \]
  whose sum is
  \[
  1+2+6+30+26+50+150+250=515.
  \]

So \(10\) should come before \(25\).

### Choice between \(50\) and \(125\)

After \(1,2,5,10,25\), both are available; since \(20<100\), putting \(50\) before \(125\) is better.

Thus the optimal order is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

## 4. Compute the maxima

Cumulative block sizes:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\max A_1 &= 1,\\
\max A_2 &= 1+1=2,\\
\max A_5 &= 1+1+4=6,\\
\max A_{10} &= 1+1+4+4=10,\\
\max A_{25} &= 10+20=30,\\
\max A_{50} &= 30+20=50,\\
\max A_{125} &= 50+100=150,\\
\max A_{250} &= 150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
M_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).
\]

We will show the minimum possible value of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} M_d
\]
is \(499\).

---

### 1. Basic structure of the sets

If \(i\mid j\), then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|.
\]
So \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

Also, since \(A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\) and both have size \(\gcd(i,j)\), we get
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

For divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\), define disjoint “new parts”
\[
B_d:=A_d\setminus \bigcup_{\substack{e\mid d\\ e<d}}A_e.
\]
Then each \(A_d\) is the disjoint union of \(B_e\) over \(e\mid d\), so by Möbius inversion (or the identity \(n=\sum_{e\mid n}\varphi(e)\)),
\[
|B_d|=\varphi(d).
\]

For the divisors of \(250\),
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250,
\]
the sizes are
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\ \varphi(2)=1,\ \varphi(5)=4,\ \varphi(10)=4,\ \varphi(25)=20,\ \varphi(50)=20,\ \varphi(125)=100,\ \varphi(250)=100.
\]

---

### 2. Turn it into an ordering problem

Think of each \(B_d\) as a block of \(\varphi(d)\) elements that first appears in \(A_d\).  
Since \(A_e\subseteq A_d\) whenever \(e\mid d\), the maxima must satisfy
\[
e\mid d \implies M_e\le M_d.
\]
So the sets \(A_d\) must be ordered in a way respecting divisibility.

If we place the blocks \(B_d\) consecutively in some linear extension of this divisibility order, then for each \(d\), the set \(A_d\) consists exactly of the blocks \(B_e\) with \(e\mid d\), all of which occur no later than \(B_d\). Hence \(M_d\) is exactly the cumulative total up to block \(B_d\).

So the problem becomes:

- jobs \(d\mid 250\),
- processing times \(p_d=\varphi(d)\),
- precedence \(e\mid d \Rightarrow e\) before \(d\),
- minimize sum of completion times.

For such a problem, among available jobs, doing the shorter one first is always optimal: if two available jobs have lengths \(x\le y\), then
\[
(T+x)+(T+x+y)\le (T+y)+(T+y+x).
\]

So we repeatedly choose the smallest available \(\varphi(d)\).

---

### 3. Optimal order

The precedence relations force:

- \(1\) first,
- then \(2\) and \(5\) become available; choose \(2\) first since \(1<4\),
- then \(5\),
- then \(10\) and \(25\) become available; choose \(10\) first since \(4<20\),
- then \(25\),
- then \(50\) and \(125\) become available; choose \(50\) first since \(20<100\),
- then \(125\),
- then \(250\).

So the optimal order is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

The completion times are the cumulative sums:
\[
1,\ 1+1=2,\ 2+4=6,\ 6+4=10,\ 10+20=30,\ 30+20=50,\ 50+100=150,\ 150+100=250.
\]

Thus
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} M_d \ge 1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

---

### 4. Achievability

Assign disjoint blocks:

- \(B_1=\{1\}\),
- \(B_2=\{2\}\),
- \(B_5=\{3,4,5,6\}\),
- \(B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\}\),
- \(B_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\}\),
- \(B_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\}\),
- \(B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\}\),
- \(B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}\),

and for all other \(n\), choose disjoint sets \(B_n\) of size \(\varphi(n)\) using integers \(>250\).

Now define for every positive integer \(n\),
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j),
\]
so the condition is satisfied.

For the divisors of \(250\), the maxima are exactly
\[
1,2,6,10,30,50,150,250,
\]
whose sum is \(499\).

Therefore the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
m_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).
\]

We want the minimum of
\[
m_1+m_2+m_5+m_{10}+m_{25}+m_{50}+m_{125}+m_{250}.
\]

## 1. Basic consequences of the condition

Given
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
setting \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=\gcd(d,n)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so \(A_d\subseteq A_n\).

Thus for divisors of \(250\), the containment relations follow divisibility.

---

## 2. A lower bound

We group the 8 divisors as
\[
1,\quad (2,5),\quad (10,25),\quad (50,125),\quad 250.
\]

### Pair \(A_2,A_5\)
We have
\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=|A_2|+|A_5|-|A_2\cap A_5|
=2+5-\gcd(2,5)=2+5-1=6.
\]
So at least one of \(m_2,m_5\) is at least \(6\). Also the other is at least \(2\) (since \(|A_2|=2\)). Hence
\[
m_2+m_5\ge 6+2=8.
\]

### Pair \(A_{10},A_{25}\)
Similarly,
\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-\gcd(10,25)=10+25-5=30.
\]
So one of \(m_{10},m_{25}\) is at least \(30\), and the other is at least \(10\). Therefore
\[
m_{10}+m_{25}\ge 30+10=40.
\]

### Pair \(A_{50},A_{125}\)
Likewise,
\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-\gcd(50,125)=50+125-25=150.
\]
So one of \(m_{50},m_{125}\) is at least \(150\), and the other is at least \(50\). Thus
\[
m_{50}+m_{125}\ge 150+50=200.
\]

### Singletons
Since \(|A_1|=1\), we have
\[
m_1\ge 1.
\]
Since \(|A_{250}|=250\), we have
\[
m_{250}\ge 250.
\]

Putting these together,
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
\ge 1+8+40+200+250=499.
\]

So the sum is at least \(499\).

---

## 3. Construction achieving 499

We now show \(499\) is attainable.

Use disjoint sets \(B_n\) with \(|B_n|=\varphi(n)\), and define
\[
A_k=\bigcup_{n\mid k} B_n.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{n\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(n)
=\gcd(i,j),
\]
so this always works.

For the divisors of \(250\), choose:
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
These sizes are
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100,
\]
which are exactly
\[
\varphi(1),\varphi(2),\varphi(5),\varphi(10),\varphi(25),\varphi(50),\varphi(125),\varphi(250).
\]

Then:
\[
\begin{aligned}
A_1&=B_1 &&\Rightarrow m_1=1,\\
A_2&=B_1\cup B_2 &&\Rightarrow m_2=2,\\
A_5&=B_1\cup B_5 &&\Rightarrow m_5=6,\\
A_{10}&=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10} &&\Rightarrow m_{10}=10,\\
A_{25}&=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25} &&\Rightarrow m_{25}=30,\\
A_{50}&=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50} &&\Rightarrow m_{50}=50,\\
A_{125}&=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125} &&\Rightarrow m_{125}=150,\\
A_{250}&=\bigcup_{d\mid 250} B_d &&\Rightarrow m_{250}=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

So the sum is
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This matches the lower bound, so it is minimal.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
S=\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d.
\]

We will show the minimum possible value is \(499\).

---

## 1. Structural facts about the sets

We are given
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad \forall i,j\ge 1.
\]

In particular, taking \(i=j=n\),
\[
|A_n|=n.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=\gcd(d,n)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so \(A_d\subseteq A_n\).

Hence for all \(i,j\),
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

---

## 2. Decompose elements by their minimal index

For each element \(x\) that appears somewhere, let \(m(x)\) be the smallest positive integer with \(x\in A_{m(x)}\).

Then \(x\in A_n\) iff \(m(x)\mid n\):

- If \(m(x)\mid n\), then \(A_{m(x)}\subseteq A_n\), so \(x\in A_n\).
- If \(x\in A_n\), then \(x\in A_{m(x)}\cap A_n=A_{\gcd(m(x),n)}\). By minimality of \(m(x)\), this forces \(\gcd(m(x),n)=m(x)\), i.e. \(m(x)\mid n\).

So if we define
\[
B_m=\{x:\ m(x)=m\},
\]
then the \(B_m\) are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{m\mid n} B_m.
\]

Thus
\[
|A_n|=\sum_{m\mid n}|B_m|=n.
\]
By Möbius inversion, this uniquely gives
\[
|B_m|=\varphi(m).
\]

---

## 3. The relevant blocks for divisors of \(250\)

Now
\[
250=2\cdot 5^3,
\]
so its divisors are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

For these \(m\), the block sizes are
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\quad \varphi(2)=1,\quad \varphi(5)=4,\quad \varphi(10)=4,
\]
\[
\varphi(25)=20,\quad \varphi(50)=20,\quad \varphi(125)=100,\quad \varphi(250)=100.
\]

So inside \(A_{250}\) we have exactly these 8 disjoint blocks, with total size
\[
1+1+4+4+20+20+100+100=250.
\]

Since \(A_{250}\) has 250 elements, to minimize maxima we should label its elements by
\[
1,2,\dots,250.
\]

---

## 4. Reformulate as a scheduling problem

Think of each block \(B_m\) as a job of length \(\varphi(m)\).  
If a block finishes at time \(C_m\), then
\[
\max A_d=\max_{m\mid d} C_m.
\]

Also, by the usual “uncrossing/deinterleaving” argument, we may assume the labels in each block are consecutive; equivalently, the 8 blocks are processed one after another in some order.

So the problem is: choose an order of these 8 jobs to minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max_{m\mid d} C_m.
\]

---

## 5. Determine the optimal order

The divisor-sets are:

- \(A_1\) uses \(B_1\)
- \(A_2\) uses \(B_1,B_2\)
- \(A_5\) uses \(B_1,B_5\)
- \(A_{10}\) uses \(B_1,B_2,B_5,B_{10}\)
- \(A_{25}\) uses \(B_1,B_5,B_{25}\)
- \(A_{50}\) uses \(B_1,B_2,B_5,B_{10},B_{25},B_{50}\)
- \(A_{125}\) uses \(B_1,B_5,B_{25},B_{125}\)
- \(A_{250}\) uses all 8 blocks.

Now choose jobs greedily by swap arguments:

### First
Only \(B_1\) can complete any divisor-set immediately (namely \(A_1\)), so \(B_1\) must come first.

### After \(B_1\)
Both \(B_2\) and \(B_5\) would each complete one new set (\(A_2\) or \(A_5\)). Since their lengths are \(1\) and \(4\), the shorter one should go first:
\[
B_2 \text{ before } B_5.
\]

### After \(B_1,B_2\)
Now \(B_5\) is the only block that can complete a new set, so it must be next.

### After \(B_1,B_2,B_5\)
Both \(B_{10}\) and \(B_{25}\) would each complete one new set (\(A_{10}\) or \(A_{25}\)). Since lengths are \(4\) and \(20\),
\[
B_{10}\text{ before }B_{25}.
\]

### After \(B_1,B_2,B_5,B_{10}\)
Only \(B_{25}\) can complete a new set, so it must be next.

### After \(B_1,B_2,B_5,B_{10},B_{25}\)
Both \(B_{50}\) and \(B_{125}\) would each complete one new set (\(A_{50}\) or \(A_{125}\)). Since lengths are \(20\) and \(100\),
\[
B_{50}\text{ before }B_{125}.
\]

Then \(B_{125}\), and finally \(B_{250}\).

So the optimal order is
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

---

## 6. Compute the maxima

Cumulative finish times are:

- \(C_1=1\)
- \(C_2=2\)
- \(C_5=6\)
- \(C_{10}=10\)
- \(C_{25}=30\)
- \(C_{50}=50\)
- \(C_{125}=150\)
- \(C_{250}=250\)

Therefore:

\[
\max A_1=1,
\]
\[
\max A_2=2,
\]
\[
\max A_5=6,
\]
\[
\max A_{10}=10,
\]
\[
\max A_{25}=30,
\]
\[
\max A_{50}=50,
\]
\[
\max A_{125}=150,
\]
\[
\max A_{250}=250.
\]

Hence
\[
S=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

---

## 7. Achievability

Take the blocks as consecutive intervals:

- \(B_1=\{1\}\)
- \(B_2=\{2\}\)
- \(B_5=\{3,4,5,6\}\)
- \(B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\}\)
- \(B_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\}\)
- \(B_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\}\)
- \(B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\}\)
- \(B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}\)

and for all other \(m\), choose disjoint sets \(B_m\) of size \(\varphi(m)\) using integers \(>250\). Then define
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{m\mid n} B_m.
\]
This satisfies
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{m\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(m)=\gcd(i,j),
\]
and gives the sum \(499\).

So the minimum is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
S=\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d,
\]
where the divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

We will find the minimum possible value of \(S\).

---

### 1. Basic consequences of the condition

We are given
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad\forall i,j\ge 1.
\]

Taking \(i=j\), we get
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid e\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_e|=\gcd(d,e)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so necessarily
\[
A_d\subseteq A_e.
\]

In particular,
\[
A_1\subset A_2\subset A_{10}\subset A_{50}\subset A_{250},
\]
and
\[
A_1\subset A_5\subset A_{25}\subset A_{125}\subset A_{250}.
\]

---

### 2. The forced block decomposition for the divisors of \(250\)

Define disjoint “new parts”:

- \(B_1=A_1\), so \(|B_1|=1\).
- \(B_2=A_2\setminus A_1\), so \(|B_2|=2-1=1\).
- \(B_5=A_5\setminus A_1\), so \(|B_5|=5-1=4\).

Since
\[
|A_2\cap A_5|=\gcd(2,5)=1=|A_1|,
\]
the sets \(B_2\) and \(B_5\) are disjoint.

Next,

- \(A_{10}\) contains \(A_2\cup A_5\), and
\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=|A_2|+|A_5|-|A_2\cap A_5|=2+5-1=6.
\]
So
\[
B_{10}=A_{10}\setminus (A_2\cup A_5),\qquad |B_{10}|=10-6=4.
\]

Similarly,

- \(A_{25}\) contains \(A_5\), and since
\[
|A_{25}\cap A_{10}|=\gcd(25,10)=5=|A_5|,
\]
the new part beyond \(A_5\) is disjoint from \(A_{10}\setminus A_5\). Thus
\[
B_{25}=A_{25}\setminus A_5,\qquad |B_{25}|=25-5=20.
\]

- \(A_{50}\) contains \(A_{10}\cup A_{25}\), and
\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-5=30,
\]
so
\[
B_{50}=A_{50}\setminus (A_{10}\cup A_{25}),\qquad |B_{50}|=50-30=20.
\]

- \(A_{125}\) contains \(A_{25}\), and
\[
|A_{125}\cap A_{50}|=\gcd(125,50)=25=|A_{25}|,
\]
so
\[
B_{125}=A_{125}\setminus A_{25},\qquad |B_{125}|=125-25=100.
\]

- \(A_{250}\) contains \(A_{50}\cup A_{125}\), and
\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-25=150,
\]
so
\[
B_{250}=A_{250}\setminus (A_{50}\cup A_{125}),\qquad |B_{250}|=250-150=100.
\]

Thus the relevant sets are unions of these disjoint blocks:
\[
\begin{aligned}
A_1&=B_1,\\
A_2&=B_1\cup B_2,\\
A_5&=B_1\cup B_5,\\
A_{10}&=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10},\\
A_{25}&=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25},\\
A_{50}&=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50},\\
A_{125}&=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125},\\
A_{250}&=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50}\cup B_{125}\cup B_{250}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Their sizes are
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100.
\]

---

### 3. We only need to order these blocks

All eight sets are contained in \(A_{250}\), which has size \(250\). To minimize maxima, we may relabel the elements of \(A_{250}\) as \(1,2,\dots,250\). Then each block should occupy a consecutive interval, and the only thing that matters is the order of the blocks.

Also:

- \(B_1\) should be first, since it belongs to every \(A_d\).
- \(B_{250}\) should be last, since it affects only \(A_{250}\), whose maximum is then forced to be \(250\) anyway.

So we need to order
\[
B_2,B_5,B_{10},B_{25},B_{50},B_{125}
\]
with the obvious precedence constraints:

- \(B_{10}\) must come after \(B_2\) and \(B_5\),
- \(B_{25}\) must come after \(B_5\),
- \(B_{50}\) must come after \(B_{10}\) and \(B_{25}\),
- \(B_{125}\) must come after \(B_{25}\).

---

### 4. Determine the optimal order

#### First choice: \(B_2\) or \(B_5\) first?

If \(B_2\) comes before \(B_5\), then
\[
\max A_2=2,\qquad \max A_5=6.
\]

If \(B_5\) comes before \(B_2\), then
\[
\max A_5=5,\qquad \max A_2=6.
\]

Since
\[
2+6<5+6,
\]
we must place \(B_2\) before \(B_5\).

So the beginning is:
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5.
\]

#### Next choice: \(B_{10}\) or \(B_{25}\)?

After \(B_1,B_2,B_5\), the next available blocks are \(B_{10}\) (size \(4\)) and \(B_{25}\) (size \(20\)).

- If \(B_{10}\) comes first, then
\[
\max A_{10}=10,\qquad \max A_{25}=30.
\]

- If \(B_{25}\) comes first, even optimally afterward, we get
\[
\max A_{25}=26,\qquad \max A_{10}=30.
\]

Since
\[
10+30<26+30,
\]
we must place \(B_{10}\) before \(B_{25}\).

So now:
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25}.
\]

#### Final choice: \(B_{50}\) or \(B_{125}\)?

Now both are available.

- If \(B_{50}\) comes first:
\[
\max A_{50}=50,\qquad \max A_{125}=150.
\]

- If \(B_{125}\) comes first:
\[
\max A_{125}=130,\qquad \max A_{50}=150.
\]

Since
\[
50+150<130+150,
\]
we must place \(B_{50}\) before \(B_{125}\).

Thus the optimal block order is
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

---

### 5. Compute the resulting maxima

Using cumulative sizes:

- \(B_1\): \(1\)
- \(B_1+B_2\): \(2\)
- \(B_1+B_2+B_5\): \(6\)
- \(+\ B_{10}\): \(10\)
- \(+\ B_{25}\): \(30\)
- \(+\ B_{50}\): \(50\)
- \(+\ B_{125}\): \(150\)
- \(+\ B_{250}\): \(250\)

So
\[
\begin{aligned}
\max A_1&=1,\\
\max A_2&=2,\\
\max A_5&=6,\\
\max A_{10}&=10,\\
\max A_{25}&=30,\\
\max A_{50}&=50,\\
\max A_{125}&=150,\\
\max A_{250}&=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
S=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

---

### 6. Achievability

This value is attainable. For example, let the blocks be

\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]

For all other \(n\), use the standard construction with disjoint blocks of size \(\varphi(n)\), defining
\[
A_m=\bigcup_{d\mid m} B_d.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j),
\]
so the condition holds.

Hence the minimum is indeed

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
D=\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\}.
\]
We only care about the sets \(A_d\) for \(d\in D\).

## 1. Basic structure

Since
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
plugging in \(i=j=d\) gives
\[
|A_d|=d.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid e\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_e|=\gcd(d,e)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so \(A_d\subseteq A_e\).

In particular, for \(d,e\in D\),
\[
A_d\cap A_e = A_{\gcd(d,e)},
\]
because \(A_{\gcd(d,e)}\subseteq A_d\cap A_e\) and both sides have size \(\gcd(d,e)\).

So the family \(\{A_d:d\in D\}\) forms exactly the divisor lattice of \(250\).

---

## 2. Decompose into disjoint “new parts”

Define \(B_d\) to be the elements that first appear at level \(d\). Then the \(B_d\) are disjoint and
\[
A_d=\bigsqcup_{e\mid d} B_e.
\]

Thus their sizes satisfy
\[
|A_d|=\sum_{e\mid d}|B_e|.
\]
Since \(|A_d|=d\), we get recursively:

- \(|B_1|=1\)
- \(|B_2|=2-1=1\)
- \(|B_5|=5-1=4\)
- \(|B_{10}|=10-(1+1+4)=4\)
- \(|B_{25}|=25-(1+4)=20\)
- \(|B_{50}|=50-(1+1+4+4+20)=20\)
- \(|B_{125}|=125-(1+4+20)=100\)
- \(|B_{250}|=250-(1+1+4+4+20+20+100)=100\)

So the block sizes are
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100.
\]

---

## 3. Interpret maxima as completion times

All relevant elements lie in \(A_{250}\), which has size \(250\). To minimize maxima, we may label its elements as \(1,2,\dots,250\). This is just choosing an order of the 250 elements.

Since all elements of the same \(B_d\) play identical roles, we may place each \(B_d\) contiguously as a block. Then \(\max A_d\) is the position of the last block among \(\{B_e:e\mid d\}\).

Now, if \(d\mid e\), every set containing \(B_e\) also contains \(B_d\). So placing \(B_e\) before \(B_d\) can never help; hence in an optimal order,
\[
d\mid e \implies B_d \text{ comes before } B_e.
\]
Thus the block order must be a linear extension of divisibility.

With such an order, \(B_d\) itself is the last block needed for \(A_d\), so
\[
\max A_d = \text{completion time of } B_d.
\]

So we must minimize the sum of completion times of the 8 blocks, under the divisibility precedence constraints.

---

## 4. Optimal block order

Among two adjacent incomparable blocks, shorter should come first (standard adjacent-swap argument for minimizing sum of completion times).

The incomparable choices are:
- \(B_2\) vs \(B_5\): sizes \(1\) and \(4\), so \(B_2\) first
- \(B_{10}\) vs \(B_{25}\): sizes \(4\) and \(20\), so \(B_{10}\) first
- \(B_{50}\) vs \(B_{125}\): sizes \(20\) and \(100\), so \(B_{50}\) first

Hence the optimal order is
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

Its completion times are the prefix sums:
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

For each positive integer \(n\), let us isolate the elements that “first appear” at level \(n\).

## 1. Structure of the family

From
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j=n\) gives
\[
|A_n|=n.
\]

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|,
\]
so \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

Hence in general,
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

Now define
\[
B_n:=A_n\setminus \bigcup_{\substack{d\mid n\\ d<n}} A_d,
\]
the elements whose minimal index is \(n\). Then the \(B_n\) are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]
Taking sizes:
\[
n=|A_n|=\sum_{d\mid n} |B_d|.
\]
So by Möbius inversion,
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n).
\]

Thus for divisors of \(250\), the relevant block sizes are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
n & 1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
\varphi(n)&1&1&4&4&20&20&100&100
\end{array}
\]

---

## 2. Reformulating the minimization

Let \(D=\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\}\).

For each \(d\in D\),
\[
A_d=\bigcup_{e\mid d} B_e.
\]

We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} \max A_d.
\]

If \(e\mid f\), then every set containing \(B_f\) also contains \(B_e\). So swapping any later element of \(B_e\) with an earlier element of \(B_f\) cannot increase any \(\max A_d\). Repeating, we may assume all elements of \(B_e\) come before all elements of \(B_f\) whenever \(e\mid f\).

Then \(\max A_d\) is exactly the last position of block \(B_d\). So we are scheduling the 8 blocks with processing times \(\varphi(d)\), subject to divisibility order, and minimizing the sum of completion times.

For two adjacent incomparable blocks of sizes \(p,q\), ordering the smaller one first is better, since
\[
(T+p)+(T+p+q) < (T+q)+(T+q+p)
\quad\text{iff}\quad p<q.
\]
So in an optimal linear extension, among available incomparable blocks, place the smaller block first.

---

## 3. Optimal order

The divisibility constraints force:

- \(1\) first,
- \(250\) last,
- \(2\) before \(10,50,250\),
- \(5\) before \(10,25,50,125,250\),
- \(10\) before \(50,250\),
- \(25\) before \(50,125,250\),
- \(50,125\) before \(250\).

Applying “shorter first” among incomparable available blocks gives the order
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

The cumulative sums are:
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} \max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

---

## 4. Achievability

Assign the blocks as consecutive intervals in that order:
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Then define \(A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d\), and place all other \(B_n\) (for \(n\nmid 250\)) after 250. This satisfies
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j),
\]
and gives the above maxima.

So the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
S=\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d.
\]

We will show the minimum possible value of \(S\) is \(499\).

---

## 1. Basic consequences of the condition

We are given
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad \forall i,j\ge 1.
\]

Taking \(i=j=n\), we get
\[
|A_n|=n.
\]

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then \(\gcd(i,j)=i\), so
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=i=|A_i|.
\]
Hence
\[
A_i\subseteq A_j \qquad (i\mid j).
\]

---

## 2. Decomposing the sets using divisors

For each positive integer \(n\), we can choose disjoint sets \(B_n\) with
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n)
\]
such that
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]

Why? Inductively, once the \(B_d\) for proper divisors \(d<n\) are chosen, they all lie inside \(A_n\), and their total size is
\[
\sum_{\substack{d\mid n\\ d<n}}\varphi(d)=n-\varphi(n),
\]
so there are exactly \(\varphi(n)\) elements of \(A_n\) left to use as \(B_n\).

Then indeed
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{d\mid i,\ d\mid j}|B_d|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(d)
=\gcd(i,j).
\]

So the problem becomes: assign labels (positive integers) to the elements of the relevant \(B_d\)'s.

---

## 3. Only the divisors of \(250\) matter

The divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

Their corresponding block sizes are
\[
|B_d|=\varphi(d):
\]
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\quad \varphi(2)=1,\quad \varphi(5)=4,\quad \varphi(10)=4,
\]
\[
\varphi(25)=20,\quad \varphi(50)=20,\quad \varphi(125)=100,\quad \varphi(250)=100.
\]

Since \(A_{250}\) has size \(250\), to minimize \(\max A_{250}\) we should clearly use the labels \(1,2,\dots,250\) for the elements of \(A_{250}\). Any other \(B_n\) with \(n\nmid 250\) can use labels \(>250\), so they do not affect the sum.

Thus we only need to order these 8 blocks inside \(\{1,\dots,250\}\).

---

## 4. Why divisors should come before multiples

Suppose \(e\mid f\), but block \(B_f\) is placed before \(B_e\). Swap them.

- Any set \(A_d\) containing \(B_f\) also contains \(B_e\), because \(f\mid d\Rightarrow e\mid d\).
- So for those sets, the latest of the two blocks is still the later one, and the maximum does not increase.
- Any set containing \(B_e\) but not \(B_f\) can only improve.

So in an optimal arrangement, whenever \(e\mid f\), block \(B_e\) must appear before block \(B_f\).

Therefore, in an optimal order, all proper divisors of \(d\) appear before \(d\), so
\[
\max A_d = \text{completion position of } B_d.
\]

Hence we now just need to minimize the sum of completion times of these 8 blocks, subject to divisibility precedence.

---

## 5. Optimal order among incomparable blocks

For two adjacent incomparable blocks of sizes \(x\) and \(y\), if current cumulative length before them is \(T\), then:

- order \(x,y\) contributes
  \[
  (T+x)+(T+x+y)=2T+2x+y,
  \]
- order \(y,x\) contributes
  \[
  (T+y)+(T+y+x)=2T+2y+x.
  \]

So \(x,y\) is better iff \(x\le y\).

Thus among currently available blocks, we should place the smaller one first.

---

## 6. Apply this to the divisor poset of \(250\)

Precedence relations are by divisibility:
\[
1\prec 2,\ 1\prec 5,\ 2\prec 10,\ 5\prec 10,\ 5\prec 25,\ 10\prec 50,\ 25\prec 50,\ 25\prec 125,\ 50\prec 250,\ 125\prec 250.
\]

Available smallest-first gives the order
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

Their sizes are
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100.
\]

So the completion times are
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\max A_1=1,\quad
\max A_2=2,\quad
\max A_5=6,\quad
\max A_{10}=10,
\]
\[
\max A_{25}=30,\quad
\max A_{50}=50,\quad
\max A_{125}=150,\quad
\max A_{250}=250.
\]

Thus
\[
S_{\min}=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

---

## 7. Achievability

Take
\[
B_1=\{1\},\quad
B_2=\{2\},\quad
B_5=\{3,4,5,6\},\quad
B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\},
\]
\[
B_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\},\quad
B_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\},
\]
\[
B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\},\quad
B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\]

For all other \(n\), choose disjoint \(B_n\) of size \(\varphi(n)\) using numbers \(>250\), and define
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]

Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(d)
=\gcd(i,j),
\]
and the above maxima are attained.

So the minimum is indeed

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
D=\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\}.
\]

We want the minimum of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d.
\]

## 1. Structural facts

From
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_n|=n.
\]

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then \(\gcd(i,j)=i\), so
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=|A_i|,
\]
hence
\[
A_i\subseteq A_j.
\]

So for divisors of \(250\), the sets are ordered by divisibility.

---

## 2. Decompose \(A_{250}\) into disjoint “types”

Every element \(x\in A_{250}\) belongs to some of the sets \(A_d\) (\(d\mid 250\)). Because membership is upward-closed under divisibility and closed under gcd, \(x\) has a unique minimal divisor \(m\mid 250\) such that
\[
x\in A_d \iff m\mid d.
\]

Let \(B_m\) be the set of elements of minimal divisor \(m\). Then the \(B_m\) are disjoint, and for every \(d\mid 250\),
\[
A_d=\bigsqcup_{m\mid d} B_m.
\]

Let \(b_m=|B_m|\). Then
\[
\sum_{m\mid d} b_m=|A_d|=d.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
b_m=\varphi(m).
\]

So for the divisors of \(250\), the block sizes are

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
m&1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
\varphi(m)&1&1&4&4&20&20&100&100
\end{array}
\]

---

## 3. Reduce to ordering these 8 blocks

Only the labels of the elements matter for maxima. In an optimal arrangement, we may place each block \(B_m\) as one consecutive interval, so the problem becomes: order these 8 blocks on the number line.

If \(m\mid d\), then putting \(B_d\) before \(B_m\) is never better: swapping them can only decrease \(\max A_m\) and does not increase any relevant maximum. Thus in an optimal order, blocks respect divisibility.

So in an optimal order, block \(B_d\) appears after all \(B_m\) with \(m\mid d\), and therefore
\[
\max A_d=\text{completion position of block }B_d.
\]

Hence we need a topological order of the divisibility poset minimizing the sum of completion times.

---

## 4. Best topological order

Among incomparable available blocks, putting the smaller block first is better, since if adjacent blocks have sizes \(p\le q\), then
\[
p+ (p+q) \le q+(q+p).
\]
So we should always place the smallest available block first.

Using sizes
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100
\]
with divisibility constraints, the optimal order is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

Their completion positions are:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\max A_1&=1,\\
\max A_2&=1+1=2,\\
\max A_5&=2+4=6,\\
\max A_{10}&=6+4=10,\\
\max A_{25}&=10+20=30,\\
\max A_{50}&=30+20=50,\\
\max A_{125}&=50+100=150,\\
\max A_{250}&=150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

---

## 5. Achievability

Take disjoint blocks:
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\},
\end{aligned}
\]
and define
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n} B_d
\]
for all \(n\) (extending with disjoint blocks of size \(\varphi(d)\) for all other \(d\)). Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j),
\]
so the condition is satisfied, and the above maxima occur.

Thus the minimum is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
g(i,j)=|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j).
\]

We want the minimum possible value of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d,
\]
where the divisors of \(250\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

---

## 1. First structural facts

Since
\[
|A_n|=|A_n\cap A_n|=\gcd(n,n)=n,
\]
we have
\[
|A_n|=n.
\]

Also, if \(g=\gcd(i,j)\), then \(g\mid i\) and \(g\mid j\), so
\[
A_g\subseteq A_i,\qquad A_g\subseteq A_j
\]
because
\[
|A_g\cap A_i|=\gcd(g,i)=g=|A_g|.
\]
Thus \(A_g\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\). But both sets have size \(g\), since
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=g=|A_g|.
\]
Therefore
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

In particular, if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
A_d=A_d\cap A_n\subseteq A_n.
\]

So the sets are nested by divisibility.

---

## 2. Decompose into disjoint blocks

Define \(B_n\) to be the set of elements that first appear in \(A_n\), i.e.
\[
B_n=A_n\setminus \bigcup_{\substack{d\mid n\\ d<n}}A_d.
\]
Then the \(B_n\) are disjoint, and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]
Hence
\[
n=|A_n|=\sum_{d\mid n}|B_d|.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n).
\]

So any valid construction is equivalent to choosing disjoint sets \(B_n\) with
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n),\qquad A_n=\bigcup_{d\mid n}B_d.
\]

For the divisors of \(250\),
\[
250=2\cdot 5^3,
\]
and the relevant block sizes are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
n & 1&2&5&10&25&50&125&250\\ \hline
\varphi(n)&1&1&4&4&20&20&100&100
\end{array}
\]

These sum to
\[
1+1+4+4+20+20+100+100=250.
\]

---

## 3. Reduce to ordering these 8 blocks

Only the blocks \(B_d\) with \(d\mid 250\) affect the sets \(A_d\) for \(d\mid 250\). Since \(A_{250}\) has size \(250\), we must have
\[
\max A_{250}\ge 250,
\]
and to minimize, we should use exactly the integers \(1,2,\dots,250\) for these 250 elements.

Now let \(L_d\) be the largest label used in block \(B_d\). Then
\[
\max A_n=\max_{d\mid n}L_d.
\]

We may assume the labels of each block are consecutive and the blocks appear one after another in increasing order of their largest labels: replacing a scattered arrangement by consecutive blocks in the same order does not increase any \(L_d\). So the problem becomes:

- arrange the 8 blocks in some order,
- each block \(B_d\) has length \(\varphi(d)\),
- minimize the sum of the completion positions corresponding to \(A_d\).

---

## 4. The block order must respect divisibility

If \(a\mid b\) but block \(B_b\) appears before \(B_a\), swap them.

- Any \(A_n\) not containing \(B_a\): unchanged.
- Any \(A_n\) containing both \(B_a\) and \(B_b\): its maximum stays the same, since the later of the two blocks still ends at the same place.
- Any \(A_n\) containing \(B_a\) but not \(B_b\): its maximum can only decrease.

So swapping cannot increase the objective.

Thus an optimal order can be assumed to satisfy:

\[
a\mid b \implies B_a \text{ comes before } B_b.
\]

In such an order, for each divisor \(d\mid 250\), all proper divisors of \(d\) occur before \(B_d\), so
\[
\max A_d = \text{completion time of block }B_d.
\]

Hence we now want to minimize the sum of completion times of these 8 jobs, with precedence given by divisibility.

---

## 5. Among incomparable blocks, shorter should come first

If two consecutive blocks \(X,Y\) are incomparable in divisibility, with lengths \(p_X>p_Y\), swapping them preserves feasibility. The total of their completion times changes from
\[
(C+p_X)+(C+p_X+p_Y)=2C+2p_X+p_Y
\]
to
\[
(C+p_Y)+(C+p_Y+p_X)=2C+2p_Y+p_X,
\]
which is smaller by \(p_X-p_Y>0\).

So in an optimal topological order, among available incomparable blocks, shorter blocks come first.

Starting from the precedence constraints, this forces the order

\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

Their lengths are
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100.
\]

So the completion times are

\[
\begin{aligned}
C_1&=1,\\
C_2&=1+1=2,\\
C_5&=2+4=6,\\
C_{10}&=6+4=10,\\
C_{25}&=10+20=30,\\
C_{50}&=30+20=50,\\
C_{125}&=50+100=150,\\
C_{250}&=150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

This is achievable, for example, by taking
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\},
\end{aligned}
\]
and assigning all other \(B_n\) disjoint numbers \(>250\).

So the minimum is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
m_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).
\]

We want the minimum possible value of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d.
\]

The divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

## 1. Basic structure of the sets

Since
\[
|A_i\cap A_i|=|A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i,
\]
we get
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=\gcd(d,n)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so \(A_d\subseteq A_n\).

Thus whenever \(g=\gcd(i,j)\), we have \(A_g\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\), and both sets have size \(g\), so
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

This is the standard “gcd family.”

---

## 2. A useful lower bound

We use the fact that if finite sets \(X,Y\subseteq \{1,2,\dots,M\}\), then
\[
|X\cup Y|\le M.
\]
So for sets \(A_r,A_s\),
\[
\max(m_r,m_s)\ge |A_r\cup A_s|.
\]

### Pair 1: \(A_2\) and \(A_5\)

\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=|A_2|+|A_5|-|A_2\cap A_5|
=2+5-\gcd(2,5)=2+5-1=6.
\]
Hence
\[
\max(m_2,m_5)\ge 6.
\]
Since \(m_2\ge 2\), we get
\[
m_2+m_5\ge 8.
\]

### Pair 2: \(A_{10}\) and \(A_{25}\)

\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-\gcd(10,25)=10+25-5=30,
\]
so
\[
\max(m_{10},m_{25})\ge 30.
\]
Since \(m_{10}\ge 10\),
\[
m_{10}+m_{25}\ge 40.
\]

### Pair 3: \(A_{50}\) and \(A_{125}\)

\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-\gcd(50,125)=50+125-25=150,
\]
so
\[
\max(m_{50},m_{125})\ge 150.
\]
Since \(m_{50}\ge 50\),
\[
m_{50}+m_{125}\ge 200.
\]

Also,
\[
m_1\ge 1,\qquad m_{250}\ge 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
= m_1+(m_2+m_5)+(m_{10}+m_{25})+(m_{50}+m_{125})+m_{250}
\ge 1+8+40+200+250=499.
\]

So the sum is at least \(499\).

---

## 3. Construction achieving 499

Now we show \(499\) is attainable.

Take disjoint blocks \(B_n\) with
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n),
\]
and define
\[
A_k=\bigcup_{d\mid k} B_d.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j),
\]
so the condition is satisfied.

For the divisors of \(250\), use these blocks:

\[
|B_1|=1,\ |B_2|=1,\ |B_5|=4,\ |B_{10}|=4,\ |B_{25}|=20,\ |B_{50}|=20,\ |B_{125}|=100,\ |B_{250}|=100.
\]

Assign them consecutive integers in this order:
\[
B_1,B_2,B_5,B_{10},B_{25},B_{50},B_{125},B_{250}.
\]

So their largest elements are at positions
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Then:
- \(A_1=B_1\), so \(m_1=1\),
- \(A_2=B_1\cup B_2\), so \(m_2=2\),
- \(A_5=B_1\cup B_5\), so \(m_5=6\),
- \(A_{10}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\), so \(m_{10}=10\),
- \(A_{25}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\), so \(m_{25}=30\),
- \(A_{50}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50}\), so \(m_{50}=50\),
- \(A_{125}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125}\), so \(m_{125}=150\),
- \(A_{250}\) contains all these blocks, so \(m_{250}=250\).

Thus
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

Since we already proved no smaller value is possible, this is the minimum.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
D=\{d:d\mid 250\}=\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\}.
\]

We want to minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d.
\]

## 1. Basic consequences of the condition

We are given
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad \forall i,j\ge1.
\]

Taking \(i=j\), we get
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid n\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=\gcd(d,n)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so \(A_d\subseteq A_n\).

Hence the sets are nested along divisibility.

---

## 2. Decompose elements by their “first appearance”

For each element \(x\), let \(m(x)\) be the smallest positive integer such that \(x\in A_{m(x)}\).

If \(m=m(x)\), then:

- since \(A_m\subseteq A_n\) whenever \(m\mid n\), we have \(x\in A_n\) for all multiples \(n\) of \(m\);
- conversely, if \(x\in A_n\), then \(x\in A_m\cap A_n=A_{\gcd(m,n)}\), and by minimality of \(m\), this forces \(\gcd(m,n)=m\), so \(m\mid n\).

Thus each element belongs to exactly those \(A_n\) with \(m(x)\mid n\).

Let
\[
B_m=\{x:m(x)=m\}.
\]
Then the \(B_m\) are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{m\mid n} B_m.
\]

So
\[
|A_n|=\sum_{m\mid n}|B_m|=n.
\]
By the standard identity \(\sum_{m\mid n}\varphi(m)=n\), we get
\[
|B_m|=\varphi(m).
\]

So for divisors of \(250\), the relevant block sizes are:

\[
\begin{array}{c|cccccccc}
m & 1 & 2 & 5 & 10 & 25 & 50 & 125 & 250\\ \hline
\varphi(m) & 1 & 1 & 4 & 4 & 20 & 20 & 100 & 100
\end{array}
\]

---

## 3. Reformulate as an ordering problem

All elements that matter for the sum lie in \(A_{250}\), which has size \(250\). So we may label those elements by \(1,2,\dots,250\).

Each block \(B_m\) has \(\varphi(m)\) elements, and
\[
A_d=\bigsqcup_{m\mid d}B_m.
\]

To minimize maxima, we should place these blocks as early as possible.

Also, if \(m\mid n\), then every set containing \(B_n\) also contains \(B_m\). So placing any element of \(B_n\) before an element of \(B_m\) can only hurt, never help. Hence in an optimal arrangement, blocks respect divisibility:
\[
m\mid n \implies B_m \text{ comes before } B_n.
\]

Thus we need a linear extension of the divisibility poset on \(D\).

If a block \(B_d\) is completed at position \(C_d\), then since all proper divisors of \(d\) come earlier, the largest label in \(A_d\) is exactly \(C_d\). So we must minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} C_d.
\]

This is now a scheduling problem with processing times \(\varphi(d)\).

---

## 4. Find the best order

The divisibility relations force:

- \(1\) first.
- After \(1\), we may choose \(2\) or \(5\). Since \(\varphi(2)=1<4=\varphi(5)\), putting \(2\) first is better.
- Then \(5\) must come.
- After \(1,2,5\), we may choose \(10\) or \(25\). Since \(\varphi(10)=4<20=\varphi(25)\), put \(10\) first.
- Then \(25\).
- After that, choose between \(50\) and \(125\). Since \(20<100\), put \(50\) first.
- Finally \(250\).

So the optimal order is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

---

## 5. Compute the completion times

Using block sizes \(1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100\):

\[
\begin{aligned}
C_1&=1,\\
C_2&=1+1=2,\\
C_5&=1+1+4=6,\\
C_{10}&=1+1+4+4=10,\\
C_{25}&=10+20=30,\\
C_{50}&=30+20=50,\\
C_{125}&=50+100=150,\\
C_{250}&=150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

---

## 6. Achievability

Take
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\},
\end{aligned}
\]
and define \(A_n=\bigsqcup_{m\mid n}B_m\) (with the remaining \(B_m\) for \(m\nmid 250\) chosen disjointly beyond \(250\), of sizes \(\varphi(m)\)). Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)
\]
holds, and the above maxima are attained.

So the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
S=\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
\]
and we want the minimum possible value of \(S\).

## 1. Basic structure of the sets

Since
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Now if \(d\mid n\), then \(\gcd(d,n)=d\), so
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=d=|A_d|.
\]
Hence
\[
A_d\subseteq A_n \qquad (d\mid n).
\]

So for divisors of \(250\), the sets are nested by divisibility.

---

## 2. Decompose \(A_{250}\) into disjoint blocks

The divisors of \(250\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

For each divisor \(t\mid 250\), let \(B_t\) be the set of elements whose *smallest* \(A_d\) (with \(d\mid 250\)) containing them is \(A_t\). Then the \(B_t\)'s are disjoint, and for every \(d\mid 250\),
\[
A_d=\bigsqcup_{t\mid d} B_t.
\]

Thus
\[
|A_d|=d=\sum_{t\mid d}|B_t|.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
|B_t|=\varphi(t).
\]

So the block sizes are:
\[
\begin{aligned}
|B_1|&=\varphi(1)=1,\\
|B_2|&=\varphi(2)=1,\\
|B_5|&=\varphi(5)=4,\\
|B_{10}|&=\varphi(10)=4,\\
|B_{25}|&=\varphi(25)=20,\\
|B_{50}|&=\varphi(50)=20,\\
|B_{125}|&=\varphi(125)=100,\\
|B_{250}|&=\varphi(250)=100.
\end{aligned}
\]

These sum to \(250\), as expected.

---

## 3. Turn the problem into an ordering problem

All elements of all \(A_d\) with \(d\mid 250\) lie in \(A_{250}\), which has size \(250\). To minimize maxima, we should use the labels
\[
1,2,\dots,250
\]
for the elements of \(A_{250}\).

Now think of placing the blocks \(B_t\) in some order along these labels.

If \(a\mid b\), then every set containing \(B_b\) also contains \(B_a\). So if an element of \(B_b\) appears before an element of \(B_a\), swapping them cannot increase any \(\max A_d\), and may decrease some. Therefore in an optimal arrangement, all of \(B_a\) comes before all of \(B_b\).

So the blocks must be ordered respecting divisibility:
\[
1\prec 2\prec 10\prec 50\prec 250,
\qquad
1\prec 5\prec 25\prec 125\prec 250,
\]
with also \(5\prec 10\) and \(25\prec 50\).

Thus we are scheduling jobs of lengths \(\varphi(t)\) with these precedence constraints, minimizing the sum of completion times. Here the completion time of block \(B_d\) is exactly \(\max A_d\).

---

## 4. Find the optimal order

Among two available incomparable blocks, the shorter one should go first: if jobs of lengths \(p<q\) are scheduled as \(q,p\), swapping them to \(p,q\) reduces the sum of their completion times by \(q-p>0\).

So at each stage, take the shortest available block.

Available sequence:

- first \(B_1\) (forced),
- then \(B_2\) and \(B_5\) are available; choose \(B_2\) since \(1<4\),
- then \(B_5\),
- then \(B_{10}\) and \(B_{25}\); choose \(B_{10}\) since \(4<20\),
- then \(B_{25}\),
- then \(B_{50}\) and \(B_{125}\); choose \(B_{50}\) since \(20<100\),
- then \(B_{125}\),
- then \(B_{250}\).

So the optimal order is
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

---

## 5. Compute the maxima

Using consecutive labels in that order:

- \(B_1\): size \(1\)  \(\Rightarrow \max A_1=1\),
- \(B_2\): size \(1\)  \(\Rightarrow \max A_2=2\),
- \(B_5\): size \(4\)  \(\Rightarrow \max A_5=6\),
- \(B_{10}\): size \(4\) \(\Rightarrow \max A_{10}=10\),
- \(B_{25}\): size \(20\) \(\Rightarrow \max A_{25}=30\),
- \(B_{50}\): size \(20\) \(\Rightarrow \max A_{50}=50\),
- \(B_{125}\): size \(100\) \(\Rightarrow \max A_{125}=150\),
- \(B_{250}\): size \(100\) \(\Rightarrow \max A_{250}=250\).

Therefore
\[
S=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This is achievable, for example by assigning:
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\},
\end{aligned}
\]
and for all other \(n\), use fresh disjoint blocks of size \(\varphi(n)\).

So the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
D=\{1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\}.
\]

We will show the minimum is \(499\).

---

### 1. Structural decomposition of the family

Since \(i\mid j\) implies
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|,
\]
we get
\[
A_i\subseteq A_j \qquad (i\mid j).
\]

Also, because \(A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\) and both sets have size \(\gcd(i,j)\), we actually have
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

So every element \(x\) belongs to all \(A_n\) with \(n\) divisible by some minimal positive integer \(d\). Let \(B_d\) be the set of elements whose minimal index is \(d\). Then the \(B_d\) are disjoint and
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]

Taking sizes:
\[
n=|A_n|=\sum_{d\mid n}|B_d|.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
|B_d|=\varphi(d).
\]

For the divisors of \(250\), the relevant block sizes are:
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\quad \varphi(2)=1,\quad \varphi(5)=4,\quad \varphi(10)=4,
\]
\[
\varphi(25)=20,\quad \varphi(50)=20,\quad \varphi(125)=100,\quad \varphi(250)=100.
\]

---

### 2. Reformulate as an ordering problem

Only the blocks \(B_d\) for \(d\mid 250\) affect the sum. Their total size is
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\varphi(d)=250.
\]
So to minimize maxima, we should use exactly the integers \(1,2,\dots,250\) for these 250 elements.

Think of each block \(B_d\) as a job of length \(\varphi(d)\). If the blocks are placed consecutively in some order, let \(C_d\) be the finishing time of block \(B_d\).

Then
\[
\max A_n=\max_{d\mid n} C_d.
\]

---

### 3. Necessary order relations

If \(a\mid b\), then every \(A_n\) containing \(B_b\) also contains \(B_a\). Hence putting \(B_b\) before \(B_a\) can never help; swapping them does not increase any \(\max A_n\), and may decrease some. Thus in an optimal arrangement,
\[
a\mid b \implies B_a \text{ comes before } B_b.
\]

So among our divisors, the order must respect divisibility.

Also, \(B_{250}\) appears only in \(A_{250}\), whose maximum is always \(250\), so \(B_{250}\) should be last.

Thus we need a linear extension of divisibility on
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

Among currently available incomparable blocks, shorter should go first, since for two adjacent jobs of lengths \(p\le q\), placing the \(p\)-job first gives smaller sum of completion times.

So the optimal order is:
\[
B_1,\ B_2,\ B_5,\ B_{10},\ B_{25},\ B_{50},\ B_{125},\ B_{250}.
\]

---

### 4. Compute the maxima

Using block lengths
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100,
\]
the finishing times are:
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Because of the divisibility order, for each \(d\in D\), the last block contributing to \(A_d\) is exactly \(B_d\). Hence
\[
\max A_1=1,\quad
\max A_2=2,\quad
\max A_5=6,\quad
\max A_{10}=10,
\]
\[
\max A_{25}=30,\quad
\max A_{50}=50,\quad
\max A_{125}=150,\quad
\max A_{250}=250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

---

### 5. Achievability

Take
\[
B_1=\{1\},\quad B_2=\{2\},\quad B_5=\{3,4,5,6\},\quad B_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\},
\]
\[
B_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\},\quad B_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\},
\]
\[
B_{125}=\{51,\dots,150\},\quad B_{250}=\{151,\dots,250\},
\]
and for all other \(d\), choose disjoint blocks \(B_d\) of size \(\varphi(d)\) using integers \(>250\). Then define
\[
A_n=\bigsqcup_{d\mid n} B_d.
\]
This satisfies \(|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\), and gives the sum \(499\).

So the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
m_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).
\]

We want the minimum possible value of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
=
m_1+m_2+m_5+m_{10}+m_{25}+m_{50}+m_{125}+m_{250}.
\]

## 1. Basic structure of the sets

Since
\[
|A_i\cap A_i|=|A_i|=\gcd(i,i)=i,
\]
we get
\[
|A_i|=i \quad \text{for every } i.
\]

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then \(\gcd(i,j)=i\), so
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=i=|A_i|.
\]
Hence \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

So in particular,
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)}.
\]

---

## 2. Lower bounds from unions

Because every element of \(A_d\) is at most \(m_d\), the union \(A_r\cup A_s\) is contained in \(\{1,2,\dots,\max(m_r,m_s)\}\). Thus
\[
|A_r\cup A_s|\le \max(m_r,m_s).
\]

We use this for three key pairs.

### Pair \(A_2, A_5\)
\[
|A_2\cup A_5|
=|A_2|+|A_5|-|A_2\cap A_5|
=2+5-\gcd(2,5)
=2+5-1=6.
\]
So
\[
\max(m_2,m_5)\ge 6.
\]
Since also \(m_2\ge 2\) and \(m_5\ge 5\), the minimum possible value of \(m_2+m_5\) is
\[
2+6=8.
\]

### Pair \(A_{10}, A_{25}\)
\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|
=10+25-\gcd(10,25)
=10+25-5=30.
\]
So
\[
\max(m_{10},m_{25})\ge 30.
\]
With \(m_{10}\ge 10,\ m_{25}\ge 25\), the minimum possible value of
\[
m_{10}+m_{25}
\]
is
\[
10+30=40.
\]

### Pair \(A_{50}, A_{125}\)
\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|
=50+125-\gcd(50,125)
=50+125-25=150.
\]
So
\[
\max(m_{50},m_{125})\ge 150.
\]
With \(m_{50}\ge 50,\ m_{125}\ge 125\), the minimum possible value of
\[
m_{50}+m_{125}
\]
is
\[
50+150=200.
\]

Also plainly,
\[
m_1\ge 1,\qquad m_{250}\ge 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} m_d
\ge 1+8+40+200+250
=499.
\]

So the sum is at least \(499\).

---

## 3. Show that \(499\) is achievable

A standard construction is to use disjoint blocks \(E_n\) of size \(\varphi(n)\), and define
\[
A_k=\bigcup_{d\mid k} E_d.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} |E_d|
=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(d)
=\gcd(i,j),
\]
as required.

For the divisors of \(250\), choose:

\[
\begin{aligned}
E_1&=\{1\},\\
E_2&=\{2\},\\
E_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
E_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
E_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\\
E_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
E_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\\
E_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
These have sizes
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100,
\]
which are exactly
\[
\varphi(1),\varphi(2),\varphi(5),\varphi(10),\varphi(25),\varphi(50),\varphi(125),\varphi(250).
\]

Then:
\[
\begin{aligned}
A_1&=E_1 &&\Rightarrow m_1=1,\\
A_2&=E_1\cup E_2 &&\Rightarrow m_2=2,\\
A_5&=E_1\cup E_5 &&\Rightarrow m_5=6,\\
A_{10}&=E_1\cup E_2\cup E_5\cup E_{10} &&\Rightarrow m_{10}=10,\\
A_{25}&=E_1\cup E_5\cup E_{25} &&\Rightarrow m_{25}=30,\\
A_{50}&=E_1\cup E_2\cup E_5\cup E_{10}\cup E_{25}\cup E_{50} &&\Rightarrow m_{50}=50,\\
A_{125}&=E_1\cup E_5\cup E_{25}\cup E_{125} &&\Rightarrow m_{125}=150,\\
A_{250}&=\bigcup_{d\mid 250}E_d &&\Rightarrow m_{250}=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This matches the lower bound, so it is minimal.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let the divisors of \(250\) be
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

We are given
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad \forall i,j\ge 1.
\]
In particular,
\[
|A_i|=|A_i\cap A_i|=i.
\]

## 1. Inclusion relations
If \(d\mid e\), then
\[
|A_d\cap A_e|=\gcd(d,e)=d=|A_d|,
\]
so \(A_d\subseteq A_e\).

Thus among the divisors of \(250\), the sets are nested according to divisibility.

## 2. The disjoint “new parts”
Define disjoint blocks corresponding to these divisors:
\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=A_1,\\
B_2&=A_2\setminus A_1,\\
B_5&=A_5\setminus A_1,\\
B_{10}&=A_{10}\setminus (A_2\cup A_5),\\
B_{25}&=A_{25}\setminus A_5,\\
B_{50}&=A_{50}\setminus (A_{10}\cup A_{25}),\\
B_{125}&=A_{125}\setminus A_{25},\\
B_{250}&=A_{250}\setminus (A_{50}\cup A_{125}).
\end{aligned}
\]
Using the intersection sizes, their cardinalities are
\[
\begin{aligned}
|B_1|&=1,\\
|B_2|&=2-1=1,\\
|B_5|&=5-1=4,\\
|B_{10}|&=10-(2+5-1)=4,\\
|B_{25}|&=25-5=20,\\
|B_{50}|&=50-(10+25-5)=20,\\
|B_{125}|&=125-25=100,\\
|B_{250}|&=250-(50+125-25)=100.
\end{aligned}
\]

So the block sizes are
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100.
\]

Also each \(A_d\) is the disjoint union of the \(B_e\) with \(e\mid d\).

---

## 3. Why divisibility order is optimal
Think of assigning positive integers to these blocks. Only the largest number in each block matters.

If a block \(B_f\) is placed before \(B_e\) where \(e\mid f\) and \(e\ne f\), swapping them cannot increase any \(\max A_d\): every set containing \(B_f\) also contains \(B_e\), while some sets contain \(B_e\) but not \(B_f\). So in an optimal arrangement, blocks appear in an order respecting divisibility.

In such an order, for each divisor \(d\), the block \(B_d\) is the last block appearing among those inside \(A_d\). Hence
\[
\max A_d=\text{(last position of }B_d\text{)}.
\]

So the problem becomes:

- schedule jobs \(1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250\),
- with processing times
  \[
  1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100,
  \]
- subject to divisibility precedence,
- minimizing the sum of completion times.

For two incomparable adjacent jobs, putting the shorter one first decreases the sum of completion times. Therefore the optimal linear extension is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

Its completion times are the cumulative sums:
\[
1,\ 2,\ 6,\ 10,\ 30,\ 50,\ 150,\ 250.
\]

Therefore
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
\ge 1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

---

## 4. Achievability
Now construct disjoint sets \(X_n\) with \(|X_n|=\varphi(n)\) for all \(n\), and for the divisors of \(250\) choose:
\[
\begin{aligned}
X_1&=\{1\},\\
X_2&=\{2\},\\
X_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
X_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
X_{25}&=\{11,\dots,30\},\\
X_{50}&=\{31,\dots,50\},\\
X_{125}&=\{51,\dots,150\},\\
X_{250}&=\{151,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Put all other \(X_n\) disjointly afterward.

Define
\[
A_i=\bigcup_{d\mid i} X_d.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\sum_{d\mid \gcd(i,j)}\varphi(d)=\gcd(i,j).
\]

For the divisors of \(250\), the maxima are exactly
\[
1,2,6,10,30,50,150,250,
\]
so the sum is \(499\).

Thus the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{499}.
\]

Let  
\[
M_d=\max A_d \qquad (d\mid 250).
\]
We want the minimum possible value of
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} M_d
= M_1+M_2+M_5+M_{10}+M_{25}+M_{50}+M_{125}+M_{250}.
\]

## 1. Basic facts

From the condition
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]
So each \(A_i\) has exactly \(i\) elements.

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|,
\]
so \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

---

## 2. Lower bounds

We will use the simple fact:

If a finite set \(S\) of positive integers has \(|S|=k\), then \(\max S\ge k\).

Also, if \(X,Y\) are sets of positive integers, then
\[
|X\cup Y|\le \max(\max X,\max Y).
\]
So a large union forces at least one of the maxima to be large.

---

### (a) \(M_1\)

Since \(|A_1|=1\),
\[
M_1\ge 1.
\]

---

### (b) \(M_2+M_5\)

We have
\[
|A_2|=2,\qquad |A_5|=5,\qquad |A_2\cap A_5|=\gcd(2,5)=1.
\]
Thus
\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=2+5-1=6.
\]
Hence
\[
\max(M_2,M_5)\ge 6.
\]
Also \(M_2\ge 2\) and \(M_5\ge 5\). Therefore the smallest possible sum is
\[
M_2+M_5\ge 2+6=8.
\]

---

### (c) \(M_{10}+M_{25}\)

Similarly,
\[
|A_{10}|=10,\qquad |A_{25}|=25,\qquad |A_{10}\cap A_{25}|=\gcd(10,25)=5.
\]
So
\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-5=30.
\]
Hence
\[
\max(M_{10},M_{25})\ge 30.
\]
With \(M_{10}\ge 10\) and \(M_{25}\ge 25\), we get
\[
M_{10}+M_{25}\ge 10+30=40.
\]

---

### (d) \(M_{50}+M_{125}\)

Likewise,
\[
|A_{50}|=50,\qquad |A_{125}|=125,\qquad |A_{50}\cap A_{125}|=\gcd(50,125)=25,
\]
so
\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-25=150.
\]
Thus
\[
\max(M_{50},M_{125})\ge 150.
\]
Since \(M_{50}\ge 50\) and \(M_{125}\ge 125\),
\[
M_{50}+M_{125}\ge 50+150=200.
\]

---

### (e) \(M_{250}\)

Since \(|A_{250}|=250\),
\[
M_{250}\ge 250.
\]

---

Adding all these lower bounds,
\[
M_1+(M_2+M_5)+(M_{10}+M_{25})+(M_{50}+M_{125})+M_{250}
\ge 1+8+40+200+250=499.
\]
So
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} M_d\ge 499.
\]

---

## 3. Construction achieving 499

Now we show 499 is attainable.

A standard construction is to choose pairwise disjoint sets \(B_n\) with
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n),
\]
and define
\[
A_i=\bigcup_{n\mid i} B_n.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
=\sum_{n\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(n)
=\gcd(i,j),
\]
since \(\sum_{d\mid m}\varphi(d)=m\).

For the divisors of \(250\), assign:

\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]
These sizes are
\[
1,1,4,4,20,20,100,100,
\]
which are exactly
\[
\varphi(1),\varphi(2),\varphi(5),\varphi(10),\varphi(25),\varphi(50),\varphi(125),\varphi(250).
\]

Then:

- \(A_1=B_1\), so \(M_1=1\).
- \(A_2=B_1\cup B_2\), so \(M_2=2\).
- \(A_5=B_1\cup B_5\), so \(M_5=6\).
- \(A_{10}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\), so \(M_{10}=10\).
- \(A_{25}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\), so \(M_{25}=30\).
- \(A_{50}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50}\), so \(M_{50}=50\).
- \(A_{125}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125}\), so \(M_{125}=150\).
- \(A_{250}\) is the union of all eight, so \(M_{250}=250\).

Thus
\[
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This matches the lower bound, so it is minimal.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

Let  
\[
S=\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d.
\]
The divisors of \(250=2\cdot 5^3\) are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]

We will find a lower bound for \(S\), then construct sets achieving it.

---

### 1. Basic facts

Since
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j),
\]
taking \(i=j\) gives
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(d\mid n\), then \(\gcd(d,n)=d\), so
\[
|A_d\cap A_n|=d=|A_d|.
\]
Hence
\[
A_d\subseteq A_n.
\]

---

### 2. A useful lower-bound trick

For any finite set \(X\) of positive integers,
\[
\max X\ge |X|.
\]
Also, for two finite sets \(X,Y\),
\[
\max(\max X,\max Y)\ge |X\cup Y|.
\]

So for two sets \(X,Y\), one of \(\max X,\max Y\) is at least \(|X\cup Y|\), and the other is at least the smaller of \(|X|,|Y|\). Thus
\[
\max X+\max Y\ge |X\cup Y|+\min(|X|,|Y|).
\]

We apply this to three pairs.

---

### 3. Lower bounds for the needed terms

Let \(m_d=\max A_d\).

#### (i) \(m_1\)

Since \(A_1\) is nonempty and contains positive integers,
\[
m_1\ge 1.
\]

#### (ii) \(m_2+m_5\)

We have
\[
|A_2|=2,\qquad |A_5|=5,\qquad |A_2\cap A_5|=\gcd(2,5)=1.
\]
So
\[
|A_2\cup A_5|=2+5-1=6.
\]
Hence
\[
m_2+m_5\ge 2+6=8.
\]

#### (iii) \(m_{10}+m_{25}\)

Similarly,
\[
|A_{10}|=10,\quad |A_{25}|=25,\quad |A_{10}\cap A_{25}|=\gcd(10,25)=5,
\]
so
\[
|A_{10}\cup A_{25}|=10+25-5=30.
\]
Thus
\[
m_{10}+m_{25}\ge 10+30=40.
\]

#### (iv) \(m_{50}+m_{125}\)

Also,
\[
|A_{50}|=50,\quad |A_{125}|=125,\quad |A_{50}\cap A_{125}|=\gcd(50,125)=25,
\]
so
\[
|A_{50}\cup A_{125}|=50+125-25=150.
\]
Thus
\[
m_{50}+m_{125}\ge 50+150=200.
\]

#### (v) \(m_{250}\)

Since \(|A_{250}|=250\),
\[
m_{250}\ge 250.
\]

---

Adding all these:
\[
S \ge 1+8+40+200+250=499.
\]

So the minimum possible value is at least \(499\).

---

### 4. Construction attaining \(499\)

Use the standard totient-block construction.

Create pairwise disjoint sets \(B_n\) with
\[
|B_n|=\varphi(n)
\]
for every positive integer \(n\), and define
\[
A_m=\bigcup_{n\mid m} B_n.
\]
Then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|
= \sum_{n\mid \gcd(i,j)} \varphi(n)
= \gcd(i,j),
\]
using \(\sum_{d\mid k}\varphi(d)=k\).

Now choose the blocks for divisors of \(250\) as consecutive intervals:

\[
\begin{aligned}
B_1&=\{1\},\\
B_2&=\{2\},\\
B_5&=\{3,4,5,6\},\\
B_{10}&=\{7,8,9,10\},\\
B_{25}&=\{11,12,\dots,30\},\\
B_{50}&=\{31,32,\dots,50\},\\
B_{125}&=\{51,52,\dots,150\},\\
B_{250}&=\{151,152,\dots,250\}.
\end{aligned}
\]

(For all other \(n\), choose disjoint \(B_n\) using integers \(>250\).)

Then:

- \(A_1=B_1\), so \(\max A_1=1\).
- \(A_2=B_1\cup B_2\), so \(\max A_2=2\).
- \(A_5=B_1\cup B_5\), so \(\max A_5=6\).
- \(A_{10}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\), so \(\max A_{10}=10\).
- \(A_{25}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\), so \(\max A_{25}=30\).
- \(A_{50}=B_1\cup B_2\cup B_5\cup B_{10}\cup B_{25}\cup B_{50}\), so \(\max A_{50}=50\).
- \(A_{125}=B_1\cup B_5\cup B_{25}\cup B_{125}\), so \(\max A_{125}=150\).
- \(A_{250}\) contains exactly \(1,\dots,250\), so \(\max A_{250}=250\).

Therefore
\[
S=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250=499.
\]

This matches the lower bound, so it is minimal.

\[
\boxed{499}
\]

We first extract the structure forced by  
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)\qquad(\forall i,j\ge1).
\]

## 1. Basic consequences

Taking \(i=j\), we get
\[
|A_i|=i.
\]

Also, if \(i\mid j\), then
\[
|A_i\cap A_j|=\gcd(i,j)=i=|A_i|,
\]
so \(A_i\subseteq A_j\).

In fact,
\[
A_i\cap A_j=A_{\gcd(i,j)},
\]
because \(A_{\gcd(i,j)}\subseteq A_i\cap A_j\) and both have size \(\gcd(i,j)\).

---

## 2. Decompose elements by their “minimal index”

For each element \(x\) appearing in some \(A_n\), let \(m(x)\) be the smallest positive integer such that \(x\in A_{m(x)}\).

Claim:  
\[
x\in A_n \iff m(x)\mid n.
\]

- If \(m(x)\mid n\), then \(A_{m(x)}\subseteq A_n\), so \(x\in A_n\).
- If \(x\in A_n\), then \(x\in A_{m(x)}\cap A_n=A_{\gcd(m(x),n)}\). By minimality of \(m(x)\), this forces \(\gcd(m(x),n)=m(x)\), so \(m(x)\mid n\).

Thus the elements split into disjoint blocks
\[
E_m:=\{x:m(x)=m\},
\]
and
\[
A_n=\bigcup_{m\mid n} E_m.
\]

Let \(c_m=|E_m|\). Then
\[
|A_n|=\sum_{m\mid n} c_m=n.
\]
By Möbius inversion,
\[
c_m=\varphi(m).
\]

So there are exactly \(\varphi(m)\) elements of type \(m\).

---

## 3. The types relevant for divisors of \(250\)

Since \(250=2\cdot 5^3\), its divisors are
\[
1,2,5,10,25,50,125,250.
\]
For these \(m\), the block sizes are
\[
\varphi(1)=1,\ \varphi(2)=1,\ \varphi(5)=4,\ \varphi(10)=4,\ \varphi(25)=20,\ \varphi(50)=20,\ \varphi(125)=100,\ \varphi(250)=100.
\]

And for each divisor \(d\mid 250\),
\[
A_d=\bigcup_{m\mid d} E_m.
\]

Since \(A_d\subseteq A_{250}\) for all \(d\mid 250\), and \(|A_{250}|=250\), to minimize the maxima we should label the \(250\) elements of \(A_{250}\) with \(1,2,\dots,250\).

So the problem becomes: order these 8 blocks of sizes above to minimize the sum of the finishing times of the sets \(A_d\).

---

## 4. Why the blocks should respect divisibility

If \(e\mid f\), then every \(A_d\) containing block \(E_f\) also contains \(E_e\).  
So if \(E_f\) appears before \(E_e\), swapping them cannot increase any \(\max A_d\). Hence in an optimal arrangement we may assume

\[
E_e \text{ appears before } E_f \quad \text{whenever } e\mid f.
\]

Thus the block order is a linear extension of the divisibility poset on the 8 divisors of \(250\).

In such an order, the latest block needed for \(A_d\) is exactly \(E_d\) itself, so \(\max A_d\) is just the completion time of block \(E_d\).

Therefore we need to minimize
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250} C_d,
\]
where job \(d\) has processing time \(\varphi(d)\), under the precedence \(e\prec f\) if \(e\mid f\).

---

## 5. Optimal order

Among available incomparable jobs, shorter processing time should go first.

Starting from the precedence constraints:

- \(1\) must be first.
- Then \(2\) and \(5\) are available; choose \(2\) first since \(1<4\).
- Then \(5\).
- Then \(10\) and \(25\) are available; choose \(10\) first since \(4<20\).
- Then \(25\).
- Then \(50\) and \(125\) are available; choose \(50\) first since \(20<100\).
- Then \(125\).
- Finally \(250\).

So the optimal block order is
\[
1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 10,\ 25,\ 50,\ 125,\ 250.
\]

Its cumulative completion times are:

\[
\begin{aligned}
C_1&=1,\\
C_2&=1+1=2,\\
C_5&=2+4=6,\\
C_{10}&=6+4=10,\\
C_{25}&=10+20=30,\\
C_{50}&=30+20=50,\\
C_{125}&=50+100=150,\\
C_{250}&=150+100=250.
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\sum_{d\mid 250}\max A_d
=1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250
=499.
\]

---

## 6. Achievability

Assign the blocks as:

- \(E_1=\{1\}\)
- \(E_2=\{2\}\)
- \(E_5=\{3,4,5,6\}\)
- \(E_{10}=\{7,8,9,10\}\)
- \(E_{25}=\{11,\dots,30\}\)
- \(E_{50}=\{31,\dots,50\}\)
